layman.simplyPut: How fast your heart beats when you're sitting still — lower is usually better.
layman.analogy: Think of your heart as a drum keeping a steady beat. At rest, a healthy heart beats about once per second — like a calm metronome.
layman.whatItTells: The number of times your heart beats per minute while at complete rest.

whenToWorry.worry: If your resting heart rate is consistently above 100 bpm while sitting still, or below 50 bpm with dizziness or fainting.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Heart rate varies naturally with stress, caffeine, and exercise. A single high reading after coffee or a stressful moment is completely normal.

interpretation.high: Tachycardia (>100 bpm at rest) may indicate stress, dehydration, anemia, thyroid disorders, infection, heart failure, or stimulant use. Persistent elevation warrants medical evaluation.
interpretation.low: Bradycardia (<60 bpm) in non-athletes may indicate conduction disorders, hypothyroidism, or medication effects (beta-blockers). In athletes, it reflects enhanced vagal tone and is typically benign.
interpretation.optimal: 60–80 bpm for most adults. Studies show RHR >80 bpm is associated with increased cardiovascular mortality.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: A resting HR that suddenly increases 10+ bpm from baseline can be an early sign of sepsis, dehydration, or PE — even before other vitals change.

high.action: See your doctor within 2 weeks
high.details: Persistent resting HR >100 bpm needs evaluation. Reduce caffeine, manage stress, check for underlying conditions like thyroid disease or anemia.

low.details: Bradycardia <60 bpm with dizziness, fatigue, or fainting needs evaluation. In athletes, low RHR is usually normal.

optimal.details: Maintain regular aerobic exercise, manage stress, and limit stimulants to keep your heart rate in a healthy range.

citations.text: Mozaffarian D, Geelen A, Brouwer IA, et al. Effect of fish oil on heart rate in humans: a meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials. Circulation. 2005;112(13):1945-1952.
DiNicolantonio JJ, Liu J, O'Keefe JH. Magnesium for the prevention and treatment of cardiovascular disease. Open Heart. 2018;5(2):e000775.
American College of Sports Medicine. ACSM's Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription. 11th ed. Wolters Kluwer; 2021.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Elevated HR + low TSH + weight loss
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Check free T4/T3; tremor, heat intolerance support diagnosis

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Elevated HR + fever + elevated WBC
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Identify source; blood cultures if systemic

drugInteractions.mechanism: Blocks beta-1 adrenergic receptors in cardiac tissue
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Target HR 60-70 bpm; avoid abrupt discontinuation

drugInteractions.mechanism: Thyroid hormone increases cardiac chronotropy
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Dose-dependent; monitor in elderly and cardiac patients

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Average resting HR 60-80 bpm. Lower values common in active individuals.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: May see slight upward trend. Increased stress and decreased activity contribute.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Resting HR may rise as cardiac efficiency declines. Monitor for arrhythmias.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Higher resting HR is common. Beta-blockers and other medications frequently alter HR.

globalRanges.pediatric: Newborn 100-160 bpm; Infant 100-150 bpm; Child 70-120 bpm; Adolescent 60-100 bpm
globalRanges.geriatric: 60-100 bpm (same as adult; reduced heart rate response to exercise)
globalRanges.pregnancy: May increase by 10-20 bpm above pre-pregnancy baseline

parameters.name: Systolic Blood Pressure
parameters.range: <120 mmHg (normal), 120–129 mmHg (elevated), 130–139 mmHg (Stage 1 HTN), ≥140 mmHg (Stage 2 HTN)

layman.simplyPut: The pressure in your arteries when your heart pumps — the top number in a blood pressure reading.
layman.analogy: Blood pressure is like water pressure in a garden hose. Systolic is the burst of pressure when the faucet first opens — the peak force your heart pumps blood with.
layman.whatItTells: The pressure exerted on artery walls when the heart contracts and pumps blood.

whenToWorry.worry: If readings are consistently above 140 mmHg, or if you get a single reading above 180 mmHg — that's a hypertensive crisis needing immediate care.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Blood pressure fluctuates throughout the day. A single slightly elevated reading at the doctor's office (white coat syndrome) is very common.

interpretation.high: Elevated SBP (≥130 mmHg) increases risk of stroke, heart attack, heart failure, and kidney disease. Each 20 mmHg increase above 115 doubles cardiovascular risk.
interpretation.low: Hypotension (SBP <90 mmHg) may cause dizziness, fainting, and organ hypoperfusion. Can indicate dehydration, blood loss, or medication effects.
interpretation.optimal: SBP <120 mmHg is optimal. The SPRINT trial showed targeting SBP <120 reduced cardiovascular events by 25% compared to <140 in high-risk patients.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: In elderly patients, wide pulse pressure (SBP-DBP >60) is a stronger predictor of cardiovascular events than either number alone — it reflects arterial stiffness.

high.action: See a doctor within 1-3 days if >140; seek emergency care if >180
high.details: Hypertensive crisis (>180 mmHg) requires immediate care. Stage 1-2 hypertension needs prompt medical management including lifestyle changes and possibly medication.

low.action: See a doctor today if symptomatic
low.details: SBP <90 mmHg with dizziness, confusion, or fainting suggests shock or severe dehydration. Increase fluid and salt intake for mild cases.

optimal.details: Continue low-sodium diet, regular exercise, stress management, and moderate alcohol intake to keep blood pressure optimal.

citations.text: Whelton PK, Carey RM, Aronow WS, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA/AAPA/ABC/ACPM/AGS/APhA/ASH/ASPC/NMA/PCNA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. Hypertension. 2018;71(6):e13-e115.
Sacks FM, Svetkey LP, Vollmer WM, et al. Effects on blood pressure of reduced dietary sodium and the Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH) diet. N Engl J Med. 2001;344(1):3-10.
Pescatello LS, Franklin BA, Fagard R, et al. Exercise and Hypertension: ACSM Position Stand. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2004;36(3):533-553.
World Health Organization. Guideline for the pharmacological treatment of hypertension in adults. WHO; 2021.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Elevated SBP + low K+ + metabolic alkalosis
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Aldosterone/renin ratio > 30 confirms

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Elevated SBP + headache + palpitations + sweating
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: 24h urine metanephrines or plasma fractionated metanephrines

drugInteractions.mechanism: Inhibits renal prostaglandins, causing sodium and water retention
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Can blunt antihypertensive efficacy; avoid chronic use

drugInteractions.mechanism: Blocks conversion of angiotensin I to II
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Monitor K+ and creatinine; contraindicated in pregnancy

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Optimal <120 mmHg. Establish baseline. Hypertension in young adults warrants secondary cause evaluation.
ageSpecificNotes.30s: Begin routine screening. Lifestyle factors (salt, weight, stress) become increasingly relevant.
ageSpecificNotes.50s: Systolic pressure rises due to arterial stiffening. Isolated systolic hypertension becomes more common.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Target typically <130 mmHg per current guidelines. Balance risk of falls from overtreatment.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: More conservative targets (< 140-150) may be appropriate in frail elderly. Orthostatic hypotension becomes a concern.

globalRanges.who: Normal <120 mmHg; Elevated 120-129; Grade 1 HTN 130-139; Grade 2 HTN ≥140 mmHg
globalRanges.asian: Japanese/Chinese guidelines use 130/80 mmHg as threshold; South Asian populations have higher CVD risk at lower BP
globalRanges.african: Higher prevalence of salt-sensitive hypertension; earlier onset, more severe target organ damage
globalRanges.pediatric: Varies by age, sex, and height percentile; hypertension defined as ≥95th percentile
globalRanges.geriatric: Target <130 mmHg per SPRINT; frail elderly may target <150 mmHg to avoid falls
globalRanges.pregnancy: Gestational hypertension: ≥140/90 mmHg after 20 weeks; preeclampsia: ≥140/90 with proteinuria

parameters.name: Diastolic Blood Pressure
parameters.range: <80 mmHg (normal), 80–89 mmHg (Stage 1 HTN), ≥90 mmHg (Stage 2 HTN)

layman.simplyPut: The pressure in your arteries between heartbeats — the bottom number in a blood pressure reading.
layman.analogy: If systolic is the squeeze, diastolic is the relaxation between squeezes — the baseline pressure in your arteries when your heart rests between beats.
layman.whatItTells: The pressure in arteries when the heart relaxes between beats.

whenToWorry.worry: Consistently above 90 mmHg, or above 120 mmHg which needs urgent care.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Diastolic pressure naturally decreases with age. A reading in the 60s-70s in older adults is often perfectly fine.

interpretation.high: Elevated DBP (≥80 mmHg) indicates increased peripheral vascular resistance. Common in younger adults with early hypertension.
interpretation.low: Low DBP (<60 mmHg) especially with high SBP indicates arterial stiffness and widened pulse pressure, a risk factor for coronary events in elderly.
interpretation.optimal: DBP <80 mmHg. In younger adults (<50 years), DBP is often a stronger predictor of cardiovascular risk than SBP.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Isolated diastolic hypertension (DBP >90 with normal SBP) in young adults under 40 is often from sympathetic overactivity and responds well to exercise and stress reduction before reaching for medications.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: DBP >90 mmHg increases cardiovascular risk. Lifestyle modifications and possibly medication are needed. DBP >120 mmHg requires urgent care.

low.details: Low DBP (<60 mmHg) in older adults may indicate arterial stiffness. Isolated low DBP is rarely an emergency unless causing symptoms.

optimal.details: Maintain balanced diet, regular exercise, and stress management to keep diastolic pressure in range.

citations.text: Zhang X, Li Y, Del Gobbo LC, et al. Effects of Magnesium Supplementation on Blood Pressure: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Double-Blind Placebo-Controlled Trials. Hypertension. 2016;68(2):324-333.
Siervo M, Lara J, Ogbonmwan I, Mathers JC. Inorganic nitrate and beetroot juice supplementation reduces blood pressure in adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Nutr. 2013;143(6):818-826.
Cornelissen VA, Smart NA. Exercise training for blood pressure: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Am Heart Assoc. 2013;2(1):e004473.
World Health Organization. Guideline for the pharmacological treatment of hypertension in adults. WHO; 2021.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Isolated elevated DBP in young adult
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Rule out secondary causes if < 30 years old

drugInteractions.mechanism: Relaxes vascular smooth muscle via L-type calcium channel blockade
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Peripheral edema is dose-dependent side effect

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Optimal <80 mmHg. Elevated diastolic in young adults needs workup.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Diastolic BP typically peaks in the 40s-50s.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Diastolic often starts to fall. A diastolic <60 mmHg may impair coronary perfusion.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Low diastolic (<60) with high systolic creates wide pulse pressure, a cardiovascular risk factor.

globalRanges.who: Normal <80 mmHg; Grade 1 HTN 80-89; Grade 2 HTN ≥90 mmHg
globalRanges.asian: Lower thresholds considered in some Asian guidelines
globalRanges.pediatric: Varies by age, sex, and height percentile
globalRanges.geriatric: DBP often decreases with age; <60 mmHg with wide pulse pressure increases risk

parameters.name: Blood Pressure Trend
parameters.range: Average home BP <135/85 mmHg; average 24-hour ABPM <130/80 mmHg

layman.simplyPut: Whether your blood pressure is going up, down, or staying steady over time.
layman.analogy: Like tracking the weather over weeks instead of checking one day — the trend tells you more than any single reading.
layman.whatItTells: A longitudinal analysis of blood pressure readings over time to identify patterns such as sustained hypertension, white-coat effect, masked hypertension, or nocturnal dipping patterns.

whenToWorry.worry: A steadily rising trend over weeks or months, especially if readings are climbing above 130/80 consistently.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Day-to-day fluctuations are normal. Morning readings are often higher than evening ones.

interpretation.high: Progressively rising trend or consistently elevated readings indicate worsening hypertension requiring medication adjustment or lifestyle intensification.
interpretation.low: Decreasing trend may reflect successful treatment, overmedication, or underlying conditions like adrenal insufficiency.
interpretation.optimal: Stable readings within normal range with appropriate nocturnal dipping (10–20% drop during sleep).

quickRef.clinicalPearl: A single elevated BP reading is less informative than the trend — ambulatory BP monitoring revealing non-dipping nocturnal BP is a stronger cardiovascular risk predictor than office readings.

high.action: Schedule a doctor visit this week
high.details: A rising blood pressure trend over weeks to months suggests worsening hypertension or medication adjustment needed.

low.action: Monitor and discuss at next visit
low.details: A downward trend may indicate effective treatment or overmedication. Ensure no symptoms of hypotension.

optimal.details: Stable blood pressure trends confirm effective lifestyle and/or medication management. Keep monitoring regularly.

citations.text: Whelton PK, Carey RM, Aronow WS, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2018;71(19):e127-e248.
Pescatello LS, et al. Physical Activity to Prevent and Treat Hypertension: A Systematic Review. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2019;51(6):1314-1323.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Progressive rise over months + weight gain
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Check waist circumference, fasting glucose, lipids

drugInteractions.mechanism: Estrogen activates RAAS and increases hepatic angiotensinogen
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Monitor BP at 3 months; switch to progestin-only if elevated

ageSpecificNotes.30s: Establish a baseline trend. Early detection of upward trajectories allows lifestyle intervention.
ageSpecificNotes.50s: Expect some upward trend in systolic. Focus on rate of change rather than absolute values.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Trends help distinguish true hypertension from white coat effects and guide medication titration.

layman.simplyPut: How much oxygen your blood is carrying — healthy lungs keep this above 95%.
layman.analogy: SpO2 measures how much oxygen your red blood cells are carrying — like checking how full the delivery trucks are on their route through your body.
layman.whatItTells: The percentage of hemoglobin binding sites occupied by oxygen in arterial blood.

whenToWorry.worry: Below 90% is a medical emergency. Between 90-94% needs same-day medical evaluation.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Readings of 95-100% are normal. Cold fingers or dark nail polish can sometimes give falsely low readings on a pulse oximeter.

interpretation.high: 100% SpO2 is normal. In COPD patients, excessive supplemental oxygen targeting >96% may suppress hypoxic respiratory drive.
interpretation.low: SpO2 <94% suggests hypoxemia requiring evaluation. <90% is a medical emergency (severe hypoxemia). 'Silent hypoxia' (low SpO2 without dyspnea) was a notable COVID-19 feature.
interpretation.optimal: 95–99% for healthy individuals at sea level.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Pulse oximetry can be falsely normal in carbon monoxide poisoning because carboxyhemoglobin absorbs light similarly to oxyhemoglobin — always get a co-oximetry ABG if CO exposure is suspected.

high.action: No action needed
high.details: SpO2 near 100% is normal. In COPD patients on oxygen, excessively high SpO2 (>96%) may warrant adjusting oxygen flow.

low.action: Call emergency services if SpO2 <90%; see a doctor today if 90-94%
low.details: SpO2 below 90% is a medical emergency suggesting severe hypoxemia. Values 90-94% need urgent evaluation for pneumonia, PE, COPD exacerbation, or heart failure.

optimal.details: Normal SpO2 95-100% indicates good oxygen exchange. Continue regular exercise to maintain lung health.

citations.text: World Health Organization. Haemoglobin concentrations for the diagnosis of anaemia and assessment of severity. WHO/NMH/NHD/MNM/11.1. Geneva: WHO; 2011.
Guyton AC, Hall JE. Textbook of Medical Physiology. 14th ed. Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 41: Transport of Oxygen and Carbon Dioxide.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Low SpO2 + normal PaO2 on ABG
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Co-oximetry differentiates; pulse ox reads ~85% in methemoglobinemia

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Low SpO2 + bilateral infiltrates on CXR
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: PaO2/FiO2 ratio < 300 defines ARDS

drugInteractions.effect: May cause methemoglobinemia, falsely low SpO2
drugInteractions.mechanism: Oxidizes hemoglobin iron to ferric state
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Treat with methylene blue if symptomatic; co-oximetry for true measurement

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Normal 96-100%. Values below 95% are unusual and warrant evaluation.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Normal may be 94-98%. Mild decline is expected. Baseline documentation is important.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Values of 93-95% may be acceptable in some patients with chronic lung disease. Know your personal baseline.

globalRanges.highAltitude: 92-95% is normal above 2500m; 85-90% may be normal above 4000m in acclimatized residents
globalRanges.pediatric: Term newborn ≥95%; preterm may be 88-94% in first days
globalRanges.geriatric: ≥94% acceptable; lower baseline in patients with chronic lung disease

parameters.name: Body Temperature
parameters.range: 97.8–99.1°F (36.6–37.3°C) orally; varies with time of day and individual

layman.simplyPut: Your body's internal heat level — a fever usually means your immune system is fighting something.
layman.analogy: Your body is like a thermostat set to about 98.6°F. Small fluctuations are normal — your temperature is actually lowest at 4 AM and highest in late afternoon.
layman.whatItTells: Core body temperature reflects the balance between heat production (metabolism, muscular activity) and heat loss.

whenToWorry.worry: Above 103°F (39.4°C) in adults, or below 95°F (35°C) which signals hypothermia. In infants under 3 months, any fever above 100.4°F needs immediate evaluation.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Normal body temperature ranges from 97-99°F and varies by time of day, activity level, and where you measure it. A reading of 99°F is not a fever.

interpretation.high: Fever (≥100.4°F / 38°C) indicates infection, inflammation, or heat illness. Hyperthermia (>104°F / 40°C) is a medical emergency.
interpretation.low: Hypothermia (<95°F / 35°C) can occur from cold exposure, sepsis, hypothyroidism, or adrenal insufficiency.
interpretation.optimal: Within personal baseline range. Track to establish your normal pattern.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: A 'normal' temperature of 98.6°F was established in 1868 — modern studies show the true average is closer to 97.9°F and declining 0.03°F per decade, likely due to reduced chronic infection burden.

high.action: Seek medical care if >103°F (39.4°C); manage mild fever at home
high.details: High fever >103°F needs evaluation for serious infection. For mild fever, rest, hydrate, and use antipyretics. Seek emergency care if >105°F.

low.details: Hypothermia <95°F is a medical emergency. Mild hypothermia needs gradual rewarming. Check for hypothyroidism, sepsis, or environmental exposure.

optimal.details: Normal body temperature 97-99°F (36.1-37.2°C) varies throughout the day and between individuals.

citations.text: Protsiv M, Ley C, Lankester J, Hastie T, Parsonnet J. Decreasing human body temperature in the United States since the Industrial Revolution. eLife. 2020;9:e49555.
Guyton AC, Hall JE. Textbook of Medical Physiology. 14th ed. Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 74: Body Temperature Regulation and Fever.
World Health Organization. Integrated Management of Childhood Illness (IMCI) chart booklet. WHO; 2014.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Fever + new heart murmur + positive blood cultures
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Duke criteria; echocardiography for vegetations

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Hypothermia + altered mental status + bradycardia
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Markedly elevated TSH, low free T4

drugInteractions.mechanism: Inhibits central COX-2 and prostaglandin E2 in hypothalamus
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Max 4g/day in healthy adults; 2g/day with liver disease

drugInteractions.mechanism: Blocks muscarinic receptors, inhibiting sweating
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Risk increases in hot environments and elderly

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Average baseline ~98.6°F but individual normal ranges vary. Establish your personal baseline.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Baseline body temperature decreases with age. Older adults may not mount a significant fever even with serious infection.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Average body temp may be 97-97.5°F. A reading of 99°F in an elderly person may represent a significant fever.

globalRanges.geriatric: Baseline may be 0.5°F (0.3°C) lower; fever threshold may need to be lowered to 99°F (37.2°C)
globalRanges.pediatric: Rectal ≥100.4°F (38°C) defines fever; higher baseline in infants and toddlers
globalRanges.pregnancy: Slightly elevated baseline (0.5-1°F above normal) in early pregnancy due to progesterone

parameters.name: Hemoglobin
parameters.range: Men: 13.5–17.5 g/dL; Women: 12.0–16.0 g/dL; Pregnant women: 11.0–14.0 g/dL

layman.simplyPut: The protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen — low levels mean anemia, which causes fatigue.
layman.analogy: Hemoglobin is the iron-rich protein in red blood cells that carries oxygen — like tiny delivery trucks transporting oxygen from your lungs to every cell in your body.
layman.whatItTells: The iron-containing protein in red blood cells responsible for carrying oxygen from the lungs to tissues and returning carbon dioxide.

whenToWorry.worry: Low hemoglobin (anemia) causing fatigue, pale skin, or shortness of breath needs evaluation. Very low levels (<7 g/dL) may need transfusion.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Hemoglobin is naturally lower in women than men, and drops slightly during pregnancy. Mild anemia from menstruation is common and often correctable with iron.

interpretation.high: Polycythemia (Hgb >17.5 men, >16 women) may indicate polycythemia vera, chronic hypoxia (COPD, high altitude), dehydration, or erythropoietin-producing tumors.
interpretation.low: Anemia (low Hgb) causes fatigue, weakness, pallor, tachycardia, and dyspnea. Causes include iron deficiency, chronic disease, B12/folate deficiency, blood loss, and bone marrow disorders.
interpretation.optimal: Mid-range for sex: Men 14–16 g/dL, Women 12.5–14.5 g/dL.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: A 'normal' hemoglobin in a dehydrated patient may mask significant anemia — always reassess after IV hydration. The true hemoglobin may be 2-3 g/dL lower.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated hemoglobin (polycythemia) increases blood viscosity and stroke risk. Check for polycythemia vera, dehydration, chronic hypoxia, or EPO-producing tumors.

low.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks; urgent if Hb <7 g/dL
low.details: Anemia causes fatigue, shortness of breath, and pallor. Severe anemia (Hb <7) may need transfusion. Identify cause: iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, chronic disease, or blood loss.

optimal.details: Normal hemoglobin reflects good iron, B12, and folate status. Continue balanced diet with iron-rich foods.

citations.text: World Health Organization. Haemoglobin concentrations for the diagnosis of anaemia and assessment of severity. WHO/NMH/NHD/MNM/11.1. Geneva: WHO; 2011.
Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 59: Anemia and Polycythemia.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Low Hgb + low MCV + low ferritin
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Ferritin < 30 ng/mL confirms; check for GI blood loss

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Low Hgb + high MCV + low B12
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Hypersegmented neutrophils on smear; check methylmalonic acid

drugInteractions.mechanism: Stimulates erythroid progenitor proliferation in bone marrow
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Target Hgb 10-11.5 g/dL in CKD; higher targets increase CV risk

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Men 14-18 g/dL, women 12-16 g/dL. Menstruation is the most common cause of low hemoglobin in young women.
ageSpecificNotes.50s: Post-menopausal women's levels equalize with men. New anemia needs investigation.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Anemia prevalence >20%. Even mild anemia is associated with falls, cognitive decline, and mortality.

globalRanges.who: Anemia: Men <13 g/dL, Women <12 g/dL, Pregnant <11 g/dL, Children 6-59mo <11 g/dL
globalRanges.african: Lower normal range by ~0.5-1 g/dL (Hb 11.5-17 men, 10.5-15 women) not due to iron deficiency
globalRanges.highAltitude: Increases 1-2 g/dL at altitudes above 2500m due to chronic hypoxia; Tibetans show less increase
globalRanges.pediatric: Newborn 14-24 g/dL; drops to nadir at 6-9 weeks (physiologic anemia of infancy 9.5-11 g/dL)
globalRanges.geriatric: WHO criteria same; some argue lower threshold for elderly is acceptable
globalRanges.pregnancy: Physiologic dilution: 11-14 g/dL normal; <11 g/dL is anemia per WHO

layman.simplyPut: The number of infection-fighting white blood cells — high can mean infection, low can mean immune weakness.
layman.analogy: White blood cells are your body's soldiers — your immune army that fights infections. More soldiers mobilize when there's an invasion (infection).
layman.whatItTells: The total number of white blood cells in the blood, reflecting the body's immune defense capacity.

whenToWorry.worry: Very high WBC (>30,000) may indicate serious infection or blood cancer. Very low WBC (<2,000) means your immune system is compromised.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: WBC naturally rises during exercise, stress, and pregnancy. A mildly elevated count during a cold is your immune system doing its job.

interpretation.high: Leukocytosis (>11 ×10³/μL) most commonly indicates infection. Also seen in inflammation, stress, corticosteroid use, and leukemia. Very high counts (>30) suggest leukemia or severe infection.
interpretation.low: Leukopenia (<4.5 ×10³/μL) increases infection risk. Causes include viral infections, autoimmune diseases, chemotherapy, aplastic anemia, and bone marrow failure.
interpretation.optimal: 5–8 ×10³/μL. Persistently elevated WBC even within normal range is associated with increased cardiovascular risk.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: A left shift (bandemia >10%) with normal or low WBC is more concerning for severe sepsis than a mildly elevated WBC without bandemia — always look at the differential.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Leukocytosis may indicate infection, inflammation, stress response, or hematologic malignancy. Very high WBC (>30,000) needs urgent hematology evaluation.

low.details: Leukopenia increases infection risk. Check for medication effects, viral infections, autoimmune conditions, or bone marrow disorders. Avoid sick contacts and practice good hygiene.

optimal.details: Normal WBC indicates a healthy immune system. Maintain good nutrition, sleep, and exercise habits.

citations.text: Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 60: Disorders of Granulocytes and Monocytes.
Nieman DC, Wentz LM. The compelling link between physical activity and the body's defense system. J Sport Health Sci. 2019;8(3):201-217.
World Health Organization. WHO Laboratory Manual for the Examination and Processing of Human Semen, 6th ed. WHO; 2021.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Elevated WBC + left shift + fever
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Band count > 10%; blood cultures + source identification

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Very high WBC (>100k) + blasts on smear
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Flow cytometry and bone marrow biopsy for classification

drugInteractions.mechanism: Releases marginated neutrophils into circulation; inhibits apoptosis
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: WBC rise of 5-20k expected; does not indicate infection

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Normal 4,500-11,000/µL. Slightly lower in some ethnic groups (benign ethnic neutropenia).
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Immune response may blunt, producing less dramatic WBC elevations during infection.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Lower baseline WBC is common. Failure to mount a WBC response to infection (normal WBC in the face of sepsis) is a poor prognostic sign.

globalRanges.african: Lower normal range (benign ethnic neutropenia): 2.5-10 ×10⁹/L vs 4.5-11 ×10⁹/L in European descent
globalRanges.pediatric: Newborn 9-30 ×10⁹/L; peaks at 6-8 hours of life; child 5-13 ×10⁹/L
globalRanges.pregnancy: Mildly elevated to 6-16 ×10⁹/L; can reach 25-30 ×10⁹/L during labor
globalRanges.geriatric: Slightly lower baseline in healthy elderly

layman.simplyPut: Tiny blood cells that help your blood clot — too few means bleeding risk, too many means clotting risk.
layman.analogy: Platelets are tiny cell fragments that act as emergency repair patches — they rush to any cut or tear in your blood vessels and form a plug to stop the bleeding.
layman.whatItTells: The number of platelets (thrombocytes) in the blood.

whenToWorry.worry: Very low platelets (<50,000) increase bleeding risk. Very high (>450,000) can increase clotting risk. Both need evaluation.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Platelet counts vary day to day. A mild fluctuation within the normal range (150,000-400,000) is nothing to worry about.

interpretation.high: Thrombocytosis (>400 ×10³/μL): reactive (infection, inflammation, iron deficiency, post-splenectomy) or primary (myeloproliferative disorders). Very high counts (>1000) may paradoxically cause bleeding.
interpretation.low: Thrombocytopenia (<150 ×10³/μL): increased risk of bleeding. Causes include ITP, HIT, DIC, liver disease, medications, and bone marrow disorders. <20 ×10³/μL risk of spontaneous bleeding.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT) paradoxically causes thrombosis, not bleeding — suspect it when platelets drop >50% within 5-10 days of heparin exposure and stop heparin immediately.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Thrombocytosis may be reactive (infection, iron deficiency, inflammation) or primary (essential thrombocythemia). High platelets increase clotting risk.

low.action: See a doctor within 1-3 days if <50,000; ER if <20,000 or bleeding
low.details: Severe thrombocytopenia (<20,000) risks spontaneous bleeding. Causes include ITP, medications, liver disease, and bone marrow disorders. Avoid NSAIDs and contact sports.

optimal.details: Normal platelet count ensures adequate clotting ability. No dietary or lifestyle changes needed.

citations.text: Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 111: Disorders of Platelets and Vessel Wall.
Guyton AC, Hall JE. Textbook of Medical Physiology. 14th ed. Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 37: Hemostasis and Blood Coagulation.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Low platelets + microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + renal failure
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: ADAMTS13 activity < 10% confirms TTP; Shiga toxin for HUS

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Isolated low platelets + recent heparin exposure
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: 4T score + anti-PF4/heparin antibodies; serotonin release assay

drugInteractions.mechanism: Antibodies against PF4-heparin complex activate platelets
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Monitor platelets q2-3 days on heparin; stop immediately if HIT suspected

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Normal 150,000-400,000/µL. ITP is more common in young women.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Myelodysplastic syndromes can cause declining platelets. Medication-related causes become more common.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Many medications (especially anticoagulants) affect platelet function even with normal counts.

globalRanges.pediatric: Newborn 150-400 ×10⁹/L; slightly wider range in neonates
globalRanges.pregnancy: Mild gestational thrombocytopenia (100-150 ×10⁹/L) in 5-10% of pregnancies

parameters.name: Fasting Blood Glucose (Self-Monitored)
parameters.range: Normal: 70–99 mg/dL; Prediabetes: 100–125 mg/dL; Diabetes: ≥126 mg/dL (confirmed on two occasions)

layman.simplyPut: Your blood sugar level after not eating overnight — high values may signal diabetes or prediabetes.
layman.analogy: Fasting glucose is your blood sugar level after not eating overnight — like checking the water level in a pool before anyone jumps in. It shows your baseline sugar management.
layman.whatItTells: Blood sugar level measured after at least 8 hours of fasting using a home glucometer.

whenToWorry.worry: Fasting glucose above 126 mg/dL on two tests indicates diabetes. Above 250 mg/dL with symptoms (excessive thirst, urination) needs prompt attention.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: A single reading of 100-110 mg/dL doesn't mean diabetes — stress, poor sleep, and medications can temporarily raise fasting glucose.

interpretation.high: Elevated fasting glucose indicates insulin resistance or insufficient insulin production. Persistent values >126 mg/dL diagnose diabetes. Acute hyperglycemia (>300 mg/dL) may indicate diabetic ketoacidosis or hyperosmolar crisis.
interpretation.low: Hypoglycemia (<70 mg/dL) causes tremor, sweating, confusion. <54 mg/dL is clinically significant. <40 mg/dL risks seizures and loss of consciousness.
interpretation.optimal: 80–100 mg/dL fasting. For diabetics on treatment, target 80–130 mg/dL per ADA guidelines.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Stress hyperglycemia in hospitalized patients without diabetes history is common and associated with worse outcomes — always recheck HbA1c and fasting glucose after discharge to determine if true diabetes is present.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Fasting glucose >126 mg/dL on two occasions diagnoses diabetes. 100-125 mg/dL indicates prediabetes. Start with diet changes, exercise, and weight management.

low.action: Eat fast-acting sugar immediately; seek care if <54 mg/dL
low.details: Hypoglycemia <70 mg/dL causes confusion, shakiness, and sweating. Eat 15g of glucose, recheck in 15 minutes. Severe hypoglycemia (<54) needs emergency care.

optimal.details: Fasting glucose 70-99 mg/dL is normal. Continue balanced diet with whole grains, fiber, and regular physical activity.

citations.text: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes—2023. Diabetes Care. 2023;46(Suppl 1):S1-S291.
Colberg SR, Sigal RJ, Yardley JE, et al. Physical Activity/Exercise and Diabetes: A Position Statement of the ADA. Diabetes Care. 2016;39(11):2065-2079.
World Health Organization. Classification of diabetes mellitus. WHO; 2019.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Fasting glucose 100-125 + HbA1c 5.7-6.4%
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: OGTT confirms impaired glucose tolerance if 2h glucose 140-199

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Fasting glucose > 126 on two occasions
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Confirm with HbA1c >= 6.5% or random glucose >= 200 + symptoms

drugInteractions.mechanism: Promotes gluconeogenesis and insulin resistance
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Steroid-induced hyperglycemia may need insulin; often resolves when stopped

labMethodology.stability: Glycolysis reduces glucose ~7%/h in whole blood at RT; separate promptly

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Normal <100 mg/dL. Fasting glucose 100-125 indicates prediabetes even in young adults.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Diabetes risk accelerates. Annual screening recommended, especially with family history or obesity.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Diabetes prevalence exceeds 25%. Fasting glucose is the simplest screening test.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Glucose targets may be relaxed (fasting <150 mg/dL acceptable in frail elderly) to avoid hypoglycemia.

globalRanges.who: Normal <110 mg/dL; Impaired fasting glucose 110-125 mg/dL; Diabetes ≥126 mg/dL (WHO uses 110 vs ADA 100 for IFG)
globalRanges.pediatric: Normal fasting glucose 70-100 mg/dL; diabetes ≥126 mg/dL same as adult

parameters.name: Hemoglobin A1c
parameters.range: Normal: <5.7%; Prediabetes: 5.7–6.4%; Diabetes: ≥6.5%

layman.simplyPut: Shows your average blood sugar over the past 3 months — like a report card for diabetes risk.
layman.analogy: HbA1c is like a 3-month average of your blood sugar — like looking at your bank statement summary instead of daily transactions. Sugar sticks to hemoglobin, and this test measures how much.
layman.whatItTells: The percentage of hemoglobin that has glucose attached, reflecting average blood glucose levels over the preceding 2–3 months.

whenToWorry.worry: HbA1c above 6.5% indicates diabetes. Above 9% means blood sugar has been poorly controlled and complications risk is high.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: HbA1c between 5.7-6.4% is prediabetes, which is often reversible with diet and exercise changes.

interpretation.high: HbA1c ≥6.5% diagnoses diabetes. Each 1% reduction in HbA1c reduces microvascular complications by ~37%. HbA1c >9% indicates very poor control requiring treatment intensification.
interpretation.low: HbA1c <4% is unusual and may indicate hemolytic conditions or laboratory error. In treated diabetics, very low HbA1c may reflect frequent hypoglycemia.
interpretation.optimal: For most diabetics: <7% (ADA target). Individualized targets of <6.5% for newly diagnosed or <8% for elderly/those with complications.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: HbA1c can be falsely low in hemolytic anemias and falsely high in iron deficiency — always correlate with fasting glucose and clinical picture. In pregnancy, use fructosamine or CGM instead.

high.action: See your doctor for diabetes management
high.details: HbA1c ≥6.5% indicates diabetes. 5.7-6.4% is prediabetes. Diet, exercise, weight loss, and possibly medication can improve glycemic control.

low.action: See your doctor if on diabetes medication
low.details: Very low HbA1c (<4%) may indicate hemolytic anemia or frequent hypoglycemia. If on insulin or sulfonylureas, medication adjustment is needed.

optimal.details: Normal HbA1c (<5.7%) reflects good blood sugar control over the past 2-3 months. Maintain balanced diet and regular exercise.

citations.text: American Diabetes Association. Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes—2023. Diabetes Care. 2023;46(Suppl 1):S1-S291.

Umpierre D, Ribeiro PA, Kramer CK, et al. Physical activity advice only or structured exercise training and association with HbA1c levels in type 2 diabetes: a systematic review and meta-analysis. JAMA. 2011;305(17):1790-1799.
World Health Organization. Use of glycated haemoglobin (HbA1c) in the diagnosis of diabetes mellitus. WHO; 2011.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: HbA1c discordant with glucose readings (falsely low/high)
differentialDiagnosis.consider: Hemoglobin variant or altered RBC lifespan
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Hemolysis, transfusion, or Hb variants (S, C, E) affect results; use fructosamine instead

drugInteractions.mechanism: Hemolytic anemia shortens RBC lifespan, reducing glycation time
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Use fructosamine or CGM for monitoring in patients on dapsone

labMethodology.stability: Stable 7 days at 4C; stable 1 week at RT for most methods

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Target <5.7%. Type 1 diabetes typically diagnosed in this age group. Tight control (A1c <7%) is critical.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Prediabetes screening (A1c 5.7-6.4%) should begin. Lifestyle intervention at this stage can prevent or delay diabetes.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Target A1c 7-7.5% for most. Benefits of tight control diminish with age.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Target A1c 7.5-8.5% for frail elderly. Avoiding hypoglycemia is more important than tight control.

globalRanges.who: Diabetes ≥6.5%; intermediate hyperglycemia 6.0-6.4% (ADA uses 5.7-6.4% for prediabetes)
globalRanges.african: May read 0.4% higher than expected for same average glucose due to glycation differences and Hb variants
globalRanges.asian: South Asians develop diabetes at lower HbA1c and BMI; earlier screening recommended
globalRanges.hispanic: May have higher HbA1c relative to glucose levels similar to African descent populations
globalRanges.pregnancy: Not recommended for gestational diabetes diagnosis; lower target <6.0% in pre-existing diabetes during pregnancy

parameters.name: Fasting Plasma Glucose (Lab)
parameters.range: Normal: 70–99 mg/dL; Impaired Fasting Glucose (prediabetes): 100–125 mg/dL; Diabetes: ≥126 mg/dL

layman.simplyPut: A lab-precise measurement of your blood sugar after fasting — the gold standard for diabetes screening.
layman.analogy: The lab version of fasting glucose — a more precise measurement than a home glucometer, drawn from a vein after 8-12 hours without food.
layman.whatItTells: A laboratory measurement of plasma glucose after a minimum 8-hour overnight fast.

whenToWorry.worry: Consistently above 126 mg/dL confirms diabetes. Between 100-125 mg/dL is prediabetes.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Lab and finger-prick values can differ by 10-15%. Lab values are the gold standard for diagnosis.

interpretation.high: FPG ≥126 mg/dL on two separate occasions diagnoses diabetes. FPG 100–125 mg/dL (impaired fasting glucose) indicates prediabetes with ~25% risk of progression to diabetes over 5 years.
interpretation.low: FPG <55 mg/dL (Whipple's triad with symptoms and resolution on glucose) evaluates for insulinoma or other causes of pathological hypoglycemia.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: The difference between self-monitored and lab fasting glucose can be significant — home glucometers allow ±15% error. Always confirm diabetes diagnosis with lab-grade venous plasma glucose.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Fasting plasma glucose ≥126 mg/dL on two tests confirms diabetes. 100-125 mg/dL is impaired fasting glucose. Lifestyle changes are first-line therapy.

low.action: Eat fast-acting sugar; seek care if persistent
low.details: Fasting glucose <70 mg/dL needs evaluation for insulinoma, adrenal insufficiency, or medication effects. Recurrent fasting hypoglycemia needs workup.

optimal.details: Normal fasting glucose indicates healthy insulin sensitivity and glucose metabolism.

citations.text: Knowler WC, Barrett-Connor E, Fowler SE, et al. Reduction in the incidence of type 2 diabetes with lifestyle intervention or metformin. N Engl J Med. 2002;346(6):393-403.
American Diabetes Association. Standards of Medical Care in Diabetes—2023. Diabetes Care. 2023;46(Suppl 1):S1-S291.
World Health Organization. Classification of diabetes mellitus. WHO; 2019.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Fasting glucose > 126 + polyuria + polydipsia + weight loss
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Type 1 vs Type 2: check C-peptide, GAD65/IA2 antibodies

drugInteractions.mechanism: Reduces hepatic glucose output; improves insulin sensitivity
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Hold before iodinated contrast; resume 48h after if renal function stable

ageSpecificNotes.30s: Begin screening at 35 per USPSTF if BMI ≥25, or earlier with risk factors.
ageSpecificNotes.50s: Screen every 3 years. Prediabetes prevalence is >30% in this age group.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Interpretation same as blood-glucose-fasting for elderly considerations.

globalRanges.pregnancy: Gestational diabetes fasting ≥92 mg/dL per IADPSG/WHO

parameters.name: Thyroid Stimulating Hormone
parameters.range: 0.4–4.0 mIU/L (some experts advocate upper limit of 2.5 mIU/L)

layman.simplyPut: Controls your thyroid gland — too high or too low affects your energy, weight, and mood.
layman.analogy: TSH is your brain's message to your thyroid gland — like a thermostat telling the furnace how hard to work. High TSH means your thyroid is underperforming, so the brain is shouting louder.
layman.whatItTells: A pituitary hormone that regulates thyroid gland function.

whenToWorry.worry: TSH above 10 with symptoms (fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance) indicates hypothyroidism needing treatment. TSH near zero suggests hyperthyroidism.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: TSH between 4.5-10 without symptoms is subclinical and may just need monitoring. Levels vary by time of day (highest in early morning).

interpretation.high: Elevated TSH (>4.0 mIU/L) with low free T4 = primary hypothyroidism. Elevated TSH with normal free T4 = subclinical hypothyroidism. Most common cause: Hashimoto's thyroiditis.
interpretation.low: Suppressed TSH (<0.4 mIU/L) with elevated free T4 = hyperthyroidism. Suppressed TSH with normal T4 = subclinical hyperthyroidism. Common causes: Graves' disease, toxic nodular goiter.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: In sick euthyroid syndrome, TSH can be low without true hyperthyroidism — don't start anti-thyroid drugs in acutely ill patients based on TSH alone. Recheck 6-8 weeks after recovery.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated TSH indicates hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid). Check free T4 to confirm. Levothyroxine replacement is the standard treatment.

low.details: Low TSH indicates hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid). Check free T4 and T3. Can cause weight loss, rapid heartbeat, anxiety, and bone loss if untreated.

optimal.details: Normal TSH indicates proper thyroid function. Recheck annually if you have risk factors for thyroid disease.

citations.text: Garber JR, Cobin RH, Gharib H, et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for Hypothyroidism in Adults. Endocr Pract. 2012;18(6):988-1028.
Jonklaas J, Bianco AC, Bauer AJ, et al. Guidelines for the Treatment of Hypothyroidism. Thyroid. 2014;24(12):1670-1751.
World Health Organization. Assessment of iodine deficiency disorders and monitoring their elimination: a guide for programme managers, 3rd ed. WHO; 2007.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Elevated TSH + low free T4 + fatigue + weight gain
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Anti-TPO antibodies positive = Hashimoto's thyroiditis

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Low TSH + high free T4 + weight loss + tremor
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: TSH receptor antibodies for Graves; thyroid uptake scan for nodular disease

drugInteractions.effect: Falsely low TSH (and falsely high free T4) on immunoassay
drugInteractions.mechanism: Biotin interferes with streptavidin-biotin immunoassay capture step
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Hold biotin 48-72h before thyroid labs; increasingly common with supplement use

labMethodology.stability: Stable 5 days refrigerated; diurnal variation (highest at night)

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Normal 0.4-4.0 mIU/L. Thyroid disease more common in women (5-8x). Screen if pregnant or planning pregnancy.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Hashimoto's thyroiditis prevalence increases. Screen with symptoms or family history.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: TSH naturally drifts higher with age. TSH 5-7 may be normal in elderly.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Some guidelines suggest higher TSH targets (4-6) in elderly to avoid overtreatment, which can cause atrial fibrillation and osteoporosis.

globalRanges.who: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L generally accepted; some populations have different ranges
globalRanges.asian: Higher upper limit of normal in iodine-sufficient Asian populations; Korean studies suggest 0.4-4.8 mIU/L
globalRanges.african: Lower TSH reference range (0.3-3.0 mIU/L) in some African populations
globalRanges.pediatric: Newborn <20 mIU/L by 48 hours; child 0.6-5.5 mIU/L; higher in neonates is normal
globalRanges.geriatric: Upper limit increases with age; 97.5th percentile ~7-8 mIU/L in adults >80
globalRanges.pregnancy: First trimester 0.1-2.5 mIU/L; second trimester 0.2-3.0; third trimester 0.3-3.0-3.5 mIU/L

parameters.name: TSH Monitoring (On Treatment)
parameters.range: Target TSH: 0.5–2.5 mIU/L for most patients on levothyroxine; 0.1–0.5 for thyroid cancer suppression therapy

layman.simplyPut: Regular checks of your thyroid hormone level while on thyroid medication — ensures proper dosing.
layman.analogy: Like regularly checking your thermostat to make sure it's keeping the house at the right temperature — ongoing TSH monitoring ensures your thyroid medication is properly dosed.
layman.whatItTells: Regular TSH monitoring for patients on thyroid hormone replacement or antithyroid medications.

whenToWorry.worry: If TSH swings significantly while on medication, your dose may need adjustment. Contact your doctor.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Minor TSH fluctuations between tests are normal. Take your thyroid medication at the same time daily for consistent results.

interpretation.high: TSH above target while on levothyroxine: dose increase needed, adherence issues, malabsorption (take on empty stomach), or medication interactions (calcium, iron, PPI).
interpretation.low: TSH below target on replacement: overtreatment risk — atrial fibrillation, osteoporosis, anxiety. Dose reduction needed.
interpretation.optimal: Within individual target range, typically 0.5–2.5 mIU/L for hypothyroidism replacement.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: After a levothyroxine dose change, wait at least 6-8 weeks before rechecking TSH — the pituitary takes that long to re-equilibrate, and earlier testing leads to unnecessary dose adjustments.

high.action: Contact your prescriber within 1-2 weeks for dose adjustment
high.details: TSH still elevated on thyroid replacement means the dose is too low. Increase levothyroxine and recheck in 6-8 weeks.

low.action: Contact your prescriber within 1-2 weeks for dose adjustment
low.details: TSH suppressed on thyroid replacement means the dose is too high. Excess thyroid hormone increases atrial fibrillation and osteoporosis risk.

optimal.details: TSH in target range on treatment. Continue current dose and recheck per your doctor's schedule.

citations.text: Jonklaas J, Bianco AC, Bauer AJ, et al. Guidelines for the Treatment of Hypothyroidism. Thyroid. 2014;24(12):1670-1751.
Garber JR, Cobin RH, Gharib H, et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for Hypothyroidism in Adults. Endocr Pract. 2012;18(6):988-1028.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: TSH rising on stable levothyroxine dose + weight gain
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Supervised levothyroxine absorption test; check calcium/iron co-administration

drugInteractions.mechanism: Chelates levothyroxine in GI tract
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Separate by 4 hours; common cause of unexplained TSH elevation

interferences: Time of levothyroxine dose (draw before morning dose)

ageSpecificNotes.30s: Pregnant women need TSH <2.5, often requiring 30-50% dose increase in first trimester.
ageSpecificNotes.50s: Medication interactions become more common. Review timing and concurrent medications at each visit.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Target TSH may be relaxed to 4-6 mIU/L. Overreplacement risks atrial fibrillation and fractures.

parameters.name: Total Cholesterol
parameters.range: Desirable: <200 mg/dL; Borderline high: 200–239 mg/dL; High: ≥240 mg/dL

layman.simplyPut: The total amount of cholesterol in your blood — a starting point, but the breakdown matters more.
layman.analogy: Total cholesterol is the sum of all cholesterol in your blood — the good (HDL), the bad (LDL), and the ugly (triglycerides/5). It's like looking at the total balance without knowing what's in savings vs debt.
layman.whatItTells: The sum of all cholesterol carried in lipoproteins — LDL, HDL, VLDL, and others.

whenToWorry.worry: Total cholesterol above 240 mg/dL is high risk. But the breakdown (LDL vs HDL) matters more than the total.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Total cholesterol can be misleadingly high if your HDL (good cholesterol) is very high. Always look at the full lipid panel.

interpretation.high: TC ≥240 mg/dL doubles cardiovascular risk compared to <200. However, high TC with very high HDL may not indicate increased risk.
interpretation.low: TC <150 mg/dL may occur in malnutrition, liver disease, hyperthyroidism, or malabsorption. Very low cholesterol (<120) associated with increased hemorrhagic stroke risk.
interpretation.optimal: <200 mg/dL with favorable LDL/HDL ratio.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: A sudden unexplained drop in total cholesterol in an elderly patient may signal occult malignancy, liver failure, or malnutrition — don't congratulate them until you've ruled these out.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Total cholesterol >240 mg/dL is high risk. Focus on reducing saturated fat, increasing fiber, exercising, and losing weight. Statin therapy may be indicated based on overall risk.

low.details: Very low total cholesterol (<120 mg/dL) may indicate malnutrition, hyperthyroidism, liver disease, or malabsorption. Most people benefit from lower cholesterol.

optimal.details: Desirable total cholesterol (<200 mg/dL) reduces cardiovascular risk. Continue balanced diet, exercise, and healthy weight.

citations.text: Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.
ACSM's Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription. 11th ed. Wolters Kluwer; 2021.
World Health Organization. Hearts: technical package for cardiovascular disease management in primary health care. WHO; 2020.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Very high total cholesterol (>300) + family history of premature CVD
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Dutch Lipid Clinic Network score; genetic testing for LDLR/APOB/PCSK9 mutations

drugInteractions.mechanism: Inhibits HMG-CoA reductase, upregulating hepatic LDL receptors
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Monitor liver enzymes at baseline; CK if myalgia develops

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Total cholesterol <200 mg/dL ideal. Screening recommended starting at age 20 every 5 years.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Cholesterol naturally rises with age. Risk assessment tools guide statin decisions.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Elevated cholesterol remains a risk factor but benefits of statin initiation after age 75 are debated.

globalRanges.asian: South Asians have higher CVD risk at lower cholesterol levels; lower thresholds may apply

parameters.name: LDL Cholesterol
parameters.range: Optimal: <100 mg/dL; Near optimal: 100–129; Borderline high: 130–159; High: 160–189; Very high: ≥190 mg/dL

layman.simplyPut: Often called 'bad' cholesterol — high levels build up in artery walls and cause heart disease.
layman.analogy: LDL is like a delivery truck dumping cholesterol into your artery walls — it builds up plaque over time. Less is better, which is why it's called 'bad' cholesterol.
layman.whatItTells: Low-density lipoprotein cholesterol is the primary driver of atherosclerotic plaque formation.

whenToWorry.worry: LDL above 190 mg/dL is very high risk. Above 130 with other risk factors needs treatment. Above 70 for people with existing heart disease.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: LDL responds well to diet changes and statins. A mildly elevated LDL is very manageable with lifestyle modifications.

interpretation.high: LDL ≥190 mg/dL warrants statin therapy regardless of other risk factors. LDL 70–189 requires risk-based decision making using pooled cohort equations.
interpretation.low: Very low LDL (<40 mg/dL) on therapy is associated with maximal CV protection. Concerns about hemorrhagic stroke at very low levels are being studied.
interpretation.optimal: <100 mg/dL for primary prevention; <70 mg/dL for secondary prevention.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Calculated LDL (Friedewald equation) is inaccurate when triglycerides >400 — use direct LDL measurement or the Martin-Hopkins equation, which performs better in hypertriglyceridemia.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated LDL is the primary target for cardiovascular risk reduction. LDL >190 needs statin therapy. Lower targets (<70) for very high-risk patients.

low.action: No action needed — lower LDL is generally better
low.details: Very low LDL (<40) is seen with some genetic conditions or medications. No clear harm demonstrated at low levels.

optimal.details: Optimal LDL (<100 mg/dL, or <70 for high-risk) significantly reduces atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk.

citations.text: Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.
Cholesterol Treatment Trialists' Collaboration. Efficacy and safety of statin therapy in older people. Lancet. 2019;393(10170):407-415.
World Health Organization. Hearts: technical package for cardiovascular disease management in primary health care. WHO; 2020.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Elevated LDL + xanthomas + premature CAD
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: LDL > 190 in adults without secondary causes strongly suggests FH

drugInteractions.mechanism: Prevents PCSK9-mediated LDL receptor degradation, increasing receptor recycling
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Injection q2-4 weeks; indicated for FH or statin-intolerant patients

labMethodology.stability: Stable 7 days refrigerated; direct LDL available non-fasting
interferences: Triglycerides > 400 invalidate Friedewald equation; use direct LDL

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Optimal <100 mg/dL. Screen for familial hypercholesterolemia if LDL >190.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: 10-year ASCVD risk calculation guides statin initiation. Consider if risk >7.5%.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Statin benefit continues. LDL targets depend on risk category: <70 for very high risk, <100 for high risk.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Continue statins if already on them. De novo initiation after 75 is individualized.

globalRanges.who: Optimal <100 mg/dL; near optimal 100-129; borderline 130-159; high 160-189; very high ≥190
globalRanges.asian: South Asians may benefit from lower LDL targets due to higher CVD risk

parameters.name: HDL Cholesterol
parameters.range: Men: >40 mg/dL; Women: >50 mg/dL; Optimal: >60 mg/dL

layman.simplyPut: Often called 'good' cholesterol — it removes excess cholesterol from arteries and brings it back to the liver.
layman.analogy: HDL is like a garbage truck that picks up excess cholesterol from your arteries and brings it back to the liver for disposal. More garbage trucks = cleaner arteries.
layman.whatItTells: High-density lipoprotein cholesterol participates in reverse cholesterol transport — moving cholesterol from arterial walls back to the liver for excretion.

whenToWorry.worry: HDL below 40 mg/dL in men or 50 mg/dL in women significantly increases heart disease risk.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: HDL is one of the harder numbers to change with medication. Exercise, moderate alcohol, and healthy fats help raise it naturally.

interpretation.high: HDL >60 mg/dL is considered a negative (protective) risk factor. Very high HDL (>100 mg/dL) may not always be protective due to potential dysfunctional HDL.
interpretation.low: Low HDL: metabolic syndrome, insulin resistance, smoking, sedentary lifestyle, high-carbohydrate diet, or genetic factors.
interpretation.optimal: >60 mg/dL.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Pharmacologically raising HDL with niacin or CETP inhibitors has consistently failed to reduce cardiovascular events — HDL is a marker of cardiovascular health, not necessarily a therapeutic target itself.

high.action: No action needed — generally protective
high.details: Very high HDL (>100 mg/dL) is usually benign but rarely may indicate CETP deficiency. Genetic causes don't always confer extra protection.

low.details: Low HDL (<40 mg/dL men, <50 women) increases cardiovascular risk. Exercise, weight loss, moderate alcohol, and smoking cessation can raise HDL. No effective HDL-raising drugs proven to reduce events.

optimal.details: Good HDL levels support reverse cholesterol transport. Regular aerobic exercise is the best way to maintain or raise HDL.

citations.text: Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.
Kodama S, Tanaka S, Saito K, et al. Effect of aerobic exercise training on serum levels of high-density lipoprotein cholesterol. Arch Intern Med. 2007;167(10):999-1008.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Very low HDL (<20) + corneal opacities + hemolytic anemia
differentialDiagnosis.consider: Tangier disease (ABCA1 mutation) or LCAT deficiency
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Genetic testing; very rare; tonsil biopsy shows cholesterol deposits in Tangier

drugInteractions.mechanism: Reduces hepatic HDL catabolism
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Flushing common; aspirin 30min prior helps; extended-release preferred

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Target >40 mg/dL for men, >50 for women. Low HDL is an independent risk factor.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: HDL tends to decline with metabolic syndrome development. Weight loss and exercise are key.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: HDL remains protective. However, pharmacologically raising HDL has not shown benefit.

globalRanges.african: Tend to have higher HDL-C (5-10 mg/dL higher on average) than European descent
globalRanges.asian: South Asians tend to have lower HDL levels

parameters.name: Triglycerides
parameters.range: Normal: <150 mg/dL; Borderline: 150–199; High: 200–499; Very high: ≥500 mg/dL

layman.simplyPut: Fats in your blood from recent meals and stored energy — high levels raise heart disease and pancreatitis risk.
layman.analogy: Triglycerides are the fat in your blood from the calories you eat but don't immediately use — like leftovers stored in your refrigerator. Too many leftovers is a problem.
layman.whatItTells: The main form of fat in the blood, carried in VLDL and chylomicrons.

whenToWorry.worry: Above 500 mg/dL risks pancreatitis (inflamed pancreas) — needs urgent treatment. Above 150 is elevated.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Triglycerides are very sensitive to what you ate recently. A high reading after a big meal or alcohol doesn't represent your true level — retest fasting.

interpretation.high: TG 200–499: increased atherogenic remnant particles. ≥500: acute pancreatitis risk requiring urgent treatment. Most common causes: diet, alcohol, obesity, uncontrolled diabetes.
interpretation.low: TG <50 mg/dL: uncommon; may indicate malabsorption, hyperthyroidism, or malnutrition.
interpretation.optimal: <100 mg/dL fasting.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Fasting is no longer strictly required for lipid panels — non-fasting triglycerides >440 mg/dL are actionable and predict cardiovascular risk as well as fasting values. But fasting is needed if triglycerides are >400.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks; urgent if >500
high.details: Triglycerides >500 mg/dL risk acute pancreatitis and need urgent treatment. Moderate elevation needs diet modification (reduce sugar, alcohol, refined carbs), exercise, and possibly medication.

low.details: Low triglycerides are not clinically significant and may indicate healthy diet and metabolism.

optimal.details: Normal triglycerides (<150 mg/dL) reflect healthy fat metabolism. Continue limiting sugar, refined carbs, and excessive alcohol.

citations.text: Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.
Miller M, Stone NJ, Ballantyne C, et al. Triglycerides and Cardiovascular Disease: A Scientific Statement From the AHA. Circulation. 2011;123(20):2292-2333.
World Health Organization. Hearts: technical package for cardiovascular disease management in primary health care. WHO; 2020.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Very high TG (>1000) + abdominal pain + lipemia retinalis
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Lipase and amylase may be falsely normal in extreme lipemia; CT abdomen

drugInteractions.mechanism: PPAR-alpha agonist increases lipoprotein lipase activity
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Monitor creatinine (may increase); avoid gemfibrozil with statins (myopathy risk)

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Optimal <100 mg/dL. High triglycerides in young adults often reflect diet (sugar, alcohol) and respond well to lifestyle changes.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Triglycerides rise with weight gain and insulin resistance. Metabolic syndrome criteria: >150 mg/dL.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Moderate elevation (150-500) primarily managed with lifestyle. >500 requires pharmacotherapy.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Role of triglyceride-lowering in elderly is less clear. Focus on pancreatitis prevention at very high levels.

globalRanges.who: Normal <150 mg/dL; borderline 150-199; high 200-499; very high ≥500
globalRanges.asian: South Asians have higher triglycerides at lower BMI (atherogenic dyslipidemia)

parameters.name: Lipid Panel Monitoring (On Treatment)
parameters.range: On high-intensity statin: expect ≥50% LDL reduction. On moderate-intensity: 30–49% reduction. Specific targets depend on risk category.

layman.simplyPut: Regular cholesterol checks while on medication — ensures your treatment is working.
layman.analogy: Regular lipid monitoring is like periodic car inspections — catching small problems before they become big ones. Usually done annually or more often if on cholesterol medication.
layman.whatItTells: Regular lipid panel monitoring for patients on lipid-lowering therapy (statins, ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, fibrates).

whenToWorry.worry: Worsening lipid trends despite medication may require dose adjustment or adding a second drug.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Lipid values fluctuate naturally by 5-10%. Small changes between tests are normal variation.

interpretation.high: Persistently elevated LDL on maximum therapy suggests non-adherence, drug interactions, familial hypercholesterolemia, or need for combination therapy.
interpretation.low: LDL <25 mg/dL on PCSK9 inhibitors has been shown to be safe in trials up to 6 years.
interpretation.optimal: LDL at or below target for risk category with stable HDL and TG.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Check CK only if a patient on a statin develops muscle symptoms — routine CK monitoring in asymptomatic patients leads to unnecessary drug discontinuation and worse cardiovascular outcomes.

high.action: Contact your prescriber within 1-2 weeks
high.details: If LDL not at target on statin therapy, dose adjustment or additional medications (ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitor) may be needed.

low.details: On treatment, very low lipid levels are generally safe. Continue monitoring for side effects (muscle pain, liver enzymes).

optimal.details: Lipid targets achieved on treatment. Continue medication adherence and healthy lifestyle. Monitor for side effects.

citations.text: Grundy SM, Stone NJ, Bailey AL, et al. 2018 AHA/ACC Guideline on the Management of Blood Cholesterol. Circulation. 2019;139(25):e1082-e1143.
ACSM's Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription. 11th ed. Wolters Kluwer; 2021.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: LDL not at goal despite max statin + lifestyle
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Verify adherence; consider PCSK9 inhibitor or ezetimibe add-on

drugInteractions.effect: Additional 15-20% LDL reduction when added to statin
drugInteractions.mechanism: Blocks NPC1L1 cholesterol transporter in intestinal brush border
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Well-tolerated; IMPROVE-IT trial showed CV benefit

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Baseline lipid panel at age 20. Repeat every 5 years if normal.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: 10-year risk assessment guides monitoring frequency. On treatment: check 4-12 weeks after initiation, then every 3-12 months.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Continue monitoring if on statins. Lipid goals may be relaxed in those with limited life expectancy.

parameters.name: Alanine Aminotransferase
parameters.range: Men: 7–56 U/L; Women: 7–45 U/L (updated guidelines suggest upper normal: 33 U/L men, 25 U/L women)

layman.simplyPut: A liver enzyme — high levels usually mean your liver cells are being damaged or inflamed.
layman.analogy: ALT is an enzyme mostly found in your liver. When liver cells are damaged, ALT leaks into your blood — like a smoke detector for liver injury.
layman.whatItTells: A liver enzyme released into the blood when hepatocytes (liver cells) are damaged.

whenToWorry.worry: ALT above 3x normal (>120 U/L) suggests significant liver inflammation. ALT above 1000 indicates acute liver injury — seek urgent care.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Mildly elevated ALT (40-60) can be caused by medications, intense exercise, fatty liver, or even a large meal. Often normalizes on its own.

interpretation.high: ALT >3× normal: significant liver injury — evaluate for hepatitis (viral, autoimmune, drug), NAFLD/NASH, alcohol. ALT >1000 U/L: acute hepatitis, acetaminophen toxicity, or ischemic hepatitis.
interpretation.low: Very low ALT (<10 U/L) is normal and not clinically significant, though very low ALT in sarcopenic elderly may reflect reduced muscle mass.
interpretation.optimal: <30 U/L in men; <20 U/L in women.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: An AST/ALT ratio >2 strongly suggests alcoholic liver disease — alcohol induces mitochondrial AST release while ALT is more cytoplasmic. Use this ratio as a noninvasive screening tool.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks; urgent if >10x normal
high.details: Elevated ALT indicates liver cell injury. Mild elevation: fatty liver, medications, alcohol. Marked elevation (>1000): acute hepatitis, toxin, or ischemia — needs urgent evaluation.

low.details: Low ALT is normal and not clinically significant.

optimal.details: Normal ALT indicates healthy liver cells. Limit alcohol, maintain healthy weight, and avoid hepatotoxic medications.

citations.text: Chalasani N, Younossi Z, Lavine JE, et al. The Diagnosis and Management of Nonalcoholic Fatty Liver Disease: Practice Guidance From the AASLD. Hepatology. 2018;67(1):328-357.
Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 337: Evaluation of Liver Function.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: ALT > 10x ULN + acute onset + jaundice
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Viral serologies (HAV IgM, HBsAg, HCV Ab); drug history; ALT/LDH ratio helps differentiate ischemic

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Mildly elevated ALT (1-3x) + obesity + metabolic syndrome
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Ultrasound for steatosis; FIB-4 score for fibrosis staging

drugInteractions.mechanism: NAPQI accumulation when glutathione depleted (overdose or chronic alcohol + therapeutic doses)
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Nomogram for acute overdose; NAC is antidote; ALT > 1000 suggests hepatocellular injury

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Normal <35 U/L for women, <40 for men. Elevated ALT in young adults increasingly reflects NAFLD.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Peak prevalence of NAFLD. Screen with ALT and consider FibroScan if elevated.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: ALT may actually decrease with age due to reduced liver mass. A 'normal' ALT in elderly may mask significant liver disease.

globalRanges.asian: Lower thresholds proposed (30 U/L men, 19 U/L women) due to NAFLD at lower BMI
globalRanges.pediatric: Upper limit of normal is lower in children (approximately 22-25 U/L)

parameters.name: Creatinine / eGFR
parameters.range: Creatinine: Men 0.7–1.3 mg/dL, Women 0.6–1.1 mg/dL; eGFR: >90 mL/min/1.73m² (normal), 60–89 (mildly decreased), 30–59 (moderately decreased), 15–29 (severely decreased), <15 (kidney failure)

layman.simplyPut: A waste product your kidneys filter out — high levels may mean your kidneys need attention.
layman.analogy: Creatinine is a waste product from muscle activity that your kidneys filter out. eGFR estimates how well your kidneys are filtering — like checking how efficiently your water filter is working.
layman.whatItTells: Serum creatinine is a waste product from muscle metabolism filtered by the kidneys.

whenToWorry.worry: eGFR below 60 for 3+ months indicates chronic kidney disease. eGFR below 15 means kidney failure. Rising creatinine is a red flag.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: eGFR naturally declines with age (~1 mL/min/year after 30). A single creatinine reading can be affected by a high-protein meal or intense exercise.

interpretation.high: Elevated creatinine (low eGFR): acute kidney injury, chronic kidney disease, dehydration, medications (ACE inhibitors, NSAIDs), rhabdomyolysis, or obstruction.
interpretation.low: Low creatinine: low muscle mass (cachexia, elderly), liver disease, or pregnancy.
interpretation.optimal: eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m² without proteinuria.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Creatinine-based eGFR overestimates kidney function in elderly, malnourished, and amputee patients due to low muscle mass — consider cystatin C-based eGFR or 24-hour urine collection in these populations.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks; urgent if rapid rise
high.details: Elevated creatinine / low eGFR indicates kidney dysfunction. eGFR <60 for 3+ months = CKD. eGFR <15 may need dialysis planning. Rapid creatinine rise is acute kidney injury — urgent evaluation.

low.details: Low creatinine reflects low muscle mass (elderly, malnutrition) or high eGFR. Not typically concerning.

optimal.details: Normal kidney function. Avoid NSAIDs, stay hydrated, control blood pressure and diabetes to preserve kidney health.

citations.text: KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease. Kidney Int Suppl. 2013;3(1):1-150.
Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 305: Chronic Kidney Disease.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Rapidly rising creatinine + oliguria + bland sediment
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: FENa > 2%; muddy brown casts on urinalysis; often post-ischemic or nephrotoxic

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Gradually declining eGFR over years + proteinuria
differentialDiagnosis.consider: Chronic kidney disease (diabetic or hypertensive nephropathy)

drugInteractions.mechanism: Reduce efferent arteriolar pressure, decreasing intraglomerular pressure
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Acceptable creatinine rise < 30% from baseline; stop if > 30% or hyperkalemia

interferences: Muscle mass (high in bodybuilders, low in elderly)
Trimethoprim/cimetidine (inhibit tubular secretion, raising creatinine without GFR change)

ageSpecificNotes.20s: eGFR >90 mL/min. Creatinine reflects muscle mass — muscular young men have higher baseline.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: eGFR normally declines ~1 mL/min/year after age 40. Faster decline is pathologic.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: eGFR 60-89 (Stage 2 CKD) is common. Monitor for progression. Avoid nephrotoxic medications.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: eGFR 45-59 is common and may be stable. Drug dosing adjustments are critical. NSAIDs should be avoided.

globalRanges.african: Race multiplier being removed from eGFR equations (CKD-EPI 2021 race-free equation now recommended); historically eGFR was multiplied by 1.16 for Black patients
globalRanges.asian: Japanese coefficient 0.813 applied; some Asian populations have different muscle mass affecting creatinine
globalRanges.pediatric: Use Schwartz equation; eGFR based on height and serum creatinine; lower creatinine normal in children
globalRanges.geriatric: eGFR naturally declines with age; ~1 mL/min/1.73m²/year after age 40; CKD staging same but prognosis differs
globalRanges.pregnancy: eGFR increases 50% due to increased renal plasma flow; creatinine drops to 0.4-0.6 mg/dL

parameters.name: Urinalysis
parameters.range: pH: 4.5–8.0; Specific gravity: 1.005–1.030; Protein: negative; Glucose: negative; Blood: negative; WBC: 0–5/HPF; RBC: 0–3/HPF; No casts, bacteria, or crystals

layman.simplyPut: A simple urine test that checks for infections, kidney disease, diabetes, and other conditions.
layman.analogy: Urinalysis is a health check using your urine — it's like reading tea leaves, except it actually works. Color, clarity, and chemical tests reveal kidney health, infections, and metabolic issues.
layman.whatItTells: A comprehensive urine screening test evaluating physical (color, clarity, specific gravity), chemical (pH, protein, glucose, blood, leukocyte esterase, nitrites, ketones, bilirubin), and microscopic (cells, casts, crystals, bacteria) properties.

whenToWorry.worry: Blood in urine (hematuria), protein in urine (proteinuria), or signs of infection (nitrites, leukocytes) need follow-up.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Urine color varies with hydration — dark yellow just means you need more water. Beets and certain medications can turn urine red harmlessly.

interpretation.high: Proteinuria: kidney disease (glomerular or tubular). Glucosuria: diabetes (glucose >180 mg/dL). Hematuria: UTI, stones, glomerulonephritis, or malignancy. WBC/nitrites: UTI.
interpretation.low: Very dilute urine (SG <1.005): excessive water intake or diabetes insipidus.
interpretation.optimal: All parameters negative/normal with appropriate concentration.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Always check the urine sediment yourself under the microscope — automated urinalysis misses casts, crystals, and dysmorphic RBCs that can change your diagnosis from 'benign hematuria' to 'glomerulonephritis.'

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks if abnormal
high.details: Abnormal urinalysis findings (protein, blood, glucose, bacteria) need follow-up. Proteinuria suggests kidney damage. Hematuria needs urological evaluation. Glucosuria suggests diabetes.

low.details: Normal urinalysis is reassuring for kidney and urinary tract health.

optimal.details: Normal urinalysis. Maintain good hydration and regular health checkups.

citations.text: KDIGO 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease. Kidney Int Suppl. 2013;3(1):1-150.
Kasper DL, Fauci AS, Hauser SL, et al. Harrison's Principles of Internal Medicine. 21st ed. McGraw-Hill; 2022. Chapter 51: Urinary Abnormalities.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Dysmorphic RBCs + RBC casts + proteinuria
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Nephrology referral; renal biopsy for definitive diagnosis

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: WBCs + nitrites + bacteria
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Urine culture for speciation and sensitivity

drugInteractions.mechanism: Drug metabolite pigment excreted in urine
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Benign; warn patients to expect color change; also discolors tears, sweat

labMethodology.stability: Examine within 2h at RT; refrigerate if delay; cells lyse in dilute/alkaline urine

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Normal: no protein, no blood, no glucose. UTI is common in young women.
ageSpecificNotes.50s: Screen for microalbuminuria in diabetics. Hematuria requires urological evaluation (rule out malignancy).
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: New hematuria must be evaluated for bladder or renal malignancy. Proteinuria predicts cardiovascular events.

globalRanges.pregnancy: Glycosuria can be normal due to lowered renal threshold; proteinuria above trace requires investigation for preeclampsia

parameters.name: Sleep Duration
parameters.range: 7–9 hours (ages 18–64); 7–8 hours (ages 65+)

layman.simplyPut: How many hours you sleep per night — most adults need 7-9 hours for optimal health.
