layman.analogy: Sleep duration is how many hours you actually sleep — your brain's nightly maintenance window. During sleep, your brain clears toxins, consolidates memories, and repairs tissues.
layman.whatItTells: The total amount of sleep obtained per 24-hour period.

whenToWorry.worry: Consistently less than 6 hours increases risk of heart disease, diabetes, obesity, and dementia. More than 9 hours may also indicate health issues.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Sleep needs vary by person (7-9 hours is typical for adults). Quality matters as much as quantity.

interpretation.high: Regularly sleeping >9 hours may indicate underlying depression, sleep disorders (hypersomnia, sleep apnea), chronic illness, or medication effects. Long sleep is associated with increased cardiovascular risk and mortality.
interpretation.low: Chronic short sleep (<6 hours) impairs cognitive performance, increases insulin resistance, raises inflammatory markers, weakens immunity, and elevates risk of obesity, diabetes, hypertension, and premature death.
interpretation.optimal: 7–8 hours consistently, with regular sleep-wake timing. Quality matters as much as duration — uninterrupted sleep with adequate deep and REM stages.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Short sleep (<6 hours) upregulates 711 genes including inflammatory pathways, while long sleep (>9 hours) has a U-shaped association with mortality — the sweet spot is 7-8 hours with consistent timing.

high.action: Discuss at next appointment if >9 hours regularly
high.details: Regularly sleeping >9 hours may indicate depression, sleep apnea with poor quality sleep, or underlying illness. Ensure sleep quality is good.

low.action: Prioritize sleep hygiene; see doctor if persistent insomnia
low.details: Chronic sleep deprivation (<6 hours) increases risks of obesity, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and cognitive decline. Practice sleep hygiene, limit screens, and maintain consistent schedule.

optimal.details: 7-9 hours of sleep supports optimal cognitive function, immune health, and metabolic regulation. Maintain consistent sleep schedule.

citations.text: Abbasi B, Kimiagar M, Sadeghniiat K, et al. The effect of magnesium supplementation on primary insomnia in elderly: A double-blind placebo-controlled clinical trial. J Res Med Sci. 2012;17(12):1161-1169.
Silber BY, Schmitt JA. Effects of tryptophan loading on human cognition, mood, and sleep. Neurosci Biobehav Rev. 2010;34(3):387-407.
Kline CE. The bidirectional relationship between exercise and sleep: Implications for exercise adherence and sleep improvement. Am J Lifestyle Med. 2014;8(6):375-379.
World Health Organization. WHO guidelines on physical activity, sedentary behaviour and sleep for children under 5 years of age. WHO; 2019.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Excessive sleepiness + loud snoring + witnessed apneas + obesity
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Polysomnography: AHI > 5 with symptoms; STOP-BANG screening tool

drugInteractions.mechanism: GABA-A receptor agonist at alpha-1 subunit promotes sleep initiation
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Short-term use only; rebound insomnia; parasomnias (sleep-driving) reported

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Recommended 7-9 hours. Sleep deprivation is endemic in this age group.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Sleep efficiency begins to decline. Sleep apnea risk increases with weight gain.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Total sleep often decreases to 6-7 hours with more nighttime awakenings. This may be normal if daytime function is preserved.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Sleep architecture changes: less deep sleep, more awakenings. Insomnia should not be treated with benzodiazepines (fall risk). CBT-I is first-line.

globalRanges.who: Adults 7-9 hours recommended; short sleep (<6h) and long sleep (>9h) both associated with health risks
globalRanges.pediatric: Newborn 14-17h; infant 12-15h; toddler 11-14h; preschool 10-13h; school-age 9-11h; teen 8-10h
globalRanges.geriatric: May need slightly less sleep (7-8h); more fragmented sleep is common

parameters.name: Mood & Stress Level
parameters.range: PSS-10 score 0–13 (low stress); PHQ-9 score 0–4 (minimal depression); GAD-7 score 0–4 (minimal anxiety)

layman.simplyPut: A self-assessment of your emotional wellbeing and stress level — affects nearly every aspect of health.
layman.analogy: Mood and stress tracking measures your mental wellbeing — like a check engine light for your mind. Chronic stress affects every body system, from heart to immune function.
layman.whatItTells: A self-reported assessment of psychological well-being, emotional state, and perceived stress burden.

whenToWorry.worry: Persistent low mood for more than 2 weeks, thoughts of self-harm, or inability to function daily warrants professional help immediately.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Temporary mood dips from life events are normal. Bad days happen — it's persistent patterns that matter, not isolated moments.

interpretation.high: High stress (PSS >20) or moderate-severe depression (PHQ-9 >10) is associated with elevated cortisol, suppressed immunity, increased cardiovascular risk, sleep disruption, and unhealthy coping behaviors.
interpretation.low: Very low stress with appropriate variability indicates healthy coping. However, persistently blunted stress response (flat affect) may indicate burnout or anhedonia.
interpretation.optimal: Low to moderate stress with effective coping strategies. Some stress (eustress) is adaptive and enhances performance and growth.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Chronic stress increases cortisol, which promotes visceral fat deposition, insulin resistance, and hippocampal atrophy — the metabolic syndrome-depression-dementia triad shares a common neuroendocrine pathway.

high.action: See your doctor or mental health provider within 1-2 weeks
high.details: High stress and mood disturbance increase cortisol, inflammation, and cardiovascular risk. Consider therapy, stress management techniques, exercise, and social support. Seek immediate help if suicidal thoughts.

low.details: Low stress and stable mood are excellent for overall health. Continue your current coping strategies.

optimal.details: Balanced mood and manageable stress levels support cardiovascular, immune, and cognitive health.

citations.text: Su KP, Tseng PT, Lin PY, et al. Association of use of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids with changes in severity of anxiety symptoms: A systematic review and meta-analysis. JAMA Netw Open. 2018;1(5):e182327.
Young LM, Pipingas A, White DJ, et al. A systematic review and meta-analysis of B vitamin supplementation on depressive symptoms, anxiety, and stress. Nutrients. 2019;11(9):2232.
Schuch FB, Vancampfort D, Richards J, et al. Exercise as a treatment for depression: A meta-analysis adjusting for publication bias. J Psychiatr Res. 2016;77:42-51.
Pascoe MC, Thompson DR, Ski CF. Yoga, mindfulness-based stress reduction and stress-related physiological measures: A meta-analysis. Psychoneuroendocrinology. 2017;86:152-168.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Persistent depressed mood + cortisol dysregulation + HPA axis hyperactivity
differentialDiagnosis.consider: Major depressive disorder with HPA axis involvement
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: PHQ-9 screening; rule out hypothyroidism, B12 deficiency, anemia as organic causes

drugInteractions.mechanism: Blocks serotonin reuptake in synaptic cleft, increasing serotonergic tone
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Black box warning for suicidality in youth; serotonin syndrome risk with MAOIs

ageSpecificNotes.20s: First episode of depression most commonly occurs in 20s. Early treatment improves long-term outcomes.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Midlife stressors (career, family, aging parents) contribute to burnout and depression.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Retirement transition, bereavement, and social isolation increase depression risk.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Depression is common but undertreated. It is often confused with dementia (pseudodementia). Screen with GDS (Geriatric Depression Scale).

parameters.name: Cortisol
parameters.range: Morning (6–8 AM): 10–20 mcg/dL (276–552 nmol/L); Evening: 3–10 mcg/dL

layman.simplyPut: Your body's main stress hormone — it should be high in the morning and low at night.
layman.analogy: Cortisol is your body's built-in alarm system hormone. It's the chemical version of a morning alarm clock — highest at dawn to wake you up, tapering through the day.
layman.whatItTells: The primary glucocorticoid stress hormone produced by the adrenal cortex.

whenToWorry.worry: Persistently high cortisol (Cushing's) causes weight gain, moon face, and weakness. Very low cortisol (Addison's) causes fatigue and low blood pressure.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Cortisol spikes naturally during stress, exercise, and illness. A single high reading during a stressful blood draw is expected.

interpretation.high: Elevated cortisol (Cushing syndrome) causes central obesity, moon face, striae, hypertension, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, and immunosuppression. Chronic stress elevates cortisol within normal range but with adverse metabolic effects.
interpretation.low: Low cortisol (adrenal insufficiency/Addison disease) causes fatigue, hypotension, hyperpigmentation, hyponatremia, and potentially life-threatening adrenal crisis.
interpretation.optimal: Normal diurnal pattern with peak in early morning and nadir at midnight. Healthy cortisol awakening response indicates adaptive HPA axis function.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Random cortisol levels are nearly useless because of pulsatile secretion and diurnal variation — use morning cortisol (>18 rules out insufficiency), midnight salivary cortisol (for Cushing's), or ACTH stimulation test.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated cortisol may indicate Cushing's syndrome, chronic stress, or exogenous steroid use. Symptoms include weight gain, moon face, and high blood sugar. Needs 24-hour urine cortisol or dexamethasone suppression test.

low.details: Low cortisol may indicate adrenal insufficiency (Addison's disease). Can cause fatigue, weight loss, hypotension, and is life-threatening in adrenal crisis. Needs urgent ACTH stimulation test.

optimal.details: Normal cortisol with healthy diurnal variation. Continue stress management, adequate sleep, and regular exercise.

citations.text: Peters EM, Anderson R, Nieman DC, Fickl H, Jogessar V. Vitamin C supplementation attenuates the increases in circulating cortisol, adrenaline and anti-inflammatory polypeptides following ultramarathon running. Int J Sports Med. 2001;22(7):537-543.
Starks MA, Starks SL, Kingsley M, Purpura M, Jäger R. The effects of phosphatidylserine on endocrine response to moderate intensity exercise. J Int Soc Sports Nutr. 2008;5:11.
Hill EE, Zack E, Battaglini C, et al. Exercise and circulating cortisol levels: the intensity threshold effect. J Endocrinol Invest. 2008;31(7):587-591.

parameters.name: Cognitive Function Score
parameters.range: MoCA ≥26/30 (normal); MMSE ≥24/30 (normal)

layman.simplyPut: A score measuring your memory, attention, and thinking speed — important to track as you age.
layman.analogy: Cognitive function tests measure your brain's processing speed, memory, and reasoning — like running diagnostics on a computer to check if all systems are performing well.
layman.whatItTells: A standardized assessment of cognitive abilities including memory, attention, executive function, language, and visuospatial skills.

whenToWorry.worry: Noticeable decline in memory, problem-solving, or language that affects daily life needs neurological evaluation. Early intervention matters.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Occasional forgetfulness (where did I put my keys?) is normal, especially with stress or poor sleep. It's different from pathological memory loss.

interpretation.high: Scores ≥26 on MoCA indicate normal cognitive function. Consistently high scores reflect cognitive reserve built through education, social engagement, and physical activity.
interpretation.low: MoCA 18–25 suggests mild cognitive impairment. MoCA <17 suggests moderate impairment. MMSE <24 suggests dementia. Further neuropsychological testing and neuroimaging warranted.
interpretation.optimal: MoCA ≥26 with stable or improving trends over time.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: The MoCA (Montreal Cognitive Assessment) detects mild cognitive impairment that the MMSE misses — if you suspect early dementia, use MoCA instead. It's more sensitive for executive dysfunction.

high.action: Excellent — continue cognitive engagement
high.details: High cognitive function scores indicate good brain health. Continue mental stimulation, exercise, and social activity.

low.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks for cognitive evaluation
low.details: Declining cognitive scores may indicate MCI, early dementia, depression, B12 deficiency, or thyroid disease. Early evaluation allows treatable causes to be addressed.

optimal.details: Normal cognitive function. Maintain exercise, sleep, social engagement, and mental stimulation to support brain health.

citations.text: Valls-Pedret C, Sala-Vila A, Serra-Mir M, et al. Mediterranean diet and age-related cognitive decline: A randomized clinical trial. JAMA Intern Med. 2015;175(7):1094-1103.

Smith AD, Smith SM, de Jager CA, et al. Homocysteine-lowering by B vitamins slows the rate of accelerated brain atrophy in mild cognitive impairment: A randomized controlled trial. PLoS One. 2010;5(9):e12244.
Livingston G, Huntley J, Sommerlad A, et al. Dementia prevention, intervention, and care: 2020 report of the Lancet Commission. Lancet. 2020;396(10248):413-446.
World Health Organization. Risk reduction of cognitive decline and dementia: WHO guidelines. WHO; 2019.

parameters.name: Reaction Time
parameters.range: Simple reaction time: 150–300 ms (visual), 120–180 ms (auditory). Choice reaction time: 300–500 ms.

layman.simplyPut: How quickly you respond to a stimulus — a measure of brain processing speed and neurological health.
layman.analogy: Reaction time measures how quickly you respond to a stimulus — like how fast you hit the brakes when a light turns red. It reflects your nervous system's processing speed.
layman.whatItTells: The time elapsed between presentation of a stimulus and the initiation of a motor response.

whenToWorry.worry: Significantly slowed reaction time may indicate neurological issues, medication side effects, or cognitive decline.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Reaction time slows slightly with age and varies with fatigue, caffeine, and attention. It's trainable with practice.

interpretation.high: Prolonged reaction time may indicate cognitive decline, sleep deprivation, medication effects, concussion, peripheral neuropathy, or neurodegenerative disease.
interpretation.low: Faster reaction times reflect better neural processing and are associated with higher cognitive function, better health, and lower mortality risk.
interpretation.optimal: Age-appropriate normal range with consistent performance and minimal variability across trials.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Reaction time increases approximately 1 ms per year after age 25 — but regular physical exercise and cognitive training can slow this decline by up to 50%, maintaining faster processing into old age.

high.action: Review medications and sleep quality
high.details: Slow reaction time may indicate fatigue, medication effects, cognitive decline, or neurological issues. Optimize sleep, review sedating medications, and stay physically active.

low.details: Fast reaction time indicates good neurological processing speed.

optimal.details: Normal reaction time indicates healthy neural processing. Continue regular exercise, adequate sleep, and cognitive engagement.

citations.text: McLellan TM, Caldwell JA, Lieberman HR. A review of caffeine's effects on cognitive, physical and occupational performance. Neurosci Biobehav Rev. 2016;71:294-312.

Rae C, Digney AL, McEwan SR, Bates TC. Oral creatine monohydrate supplementation improves brain performance: a double-blind, placebo-controlled, cross-over trial. Proc Biol Sci. 2003;270(1529):2147-2150.

Dupuy O, Gauthier CJ, Fraser SA, et al. Higher levels of cardiovascular fitness are associated with better executive function and prefrontal oxygenation in younger and older women. Front Hum Neurosci. 2015;9:66.

parameters.name: C-Reactive Protein
parameters.range: hs-CRP <1.0 mg/L (low cardiovascular risk); 1.0–3.0 mg/L (moderate risk); >3.0 mg/L (high risk). Standard CRP <10 mg/L.

layman.simplyPut: A blood protein that rises with inflammation anywhere in your body — high levels raise heart disease risk too.
layman.analogy: CRP is your body's fire alarm for inflammation — it rises rapidly when there's infection, injury, or chronic inflammation anywhere in your body.
layman.whatItTells: An acute-phase protein produced by the liver in response to inflammation.

whenToWorry.worry: CRP above 10 mg/L suggests significant infection or inflammation. High-sensitivity CRP (hs-CRP) above 3 mg/L indicates elevated cardiovascular risk.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: CRP spikes with any infection, even a common cold. One elevated reading during illness doesn't predict future heart disease.

interpretation.high: hs-CRP >3.0 mg/L indicates high cardiovascular risk even with normal cholesterol. Very high CRP (>10 mg/L) indicates acute infection, autoimmune flare, or tissue injury. Chronic mild elevation drives atherosclerosis, insulin resistance, and cancer risk.
interpretation.low: hs-CRP <1.0 mg/L indicates low inflammatory burden and lower cardiovascular risk.
interpretation.optimal: hs-CRP <1.0 mg/L. The JUPITER trial showed statin therapy in patients with elevated CRP but normal LDL reduced cardiovascular events by 44%.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: hsCRP <1 mg/L, 1-3, and >3 stratify cardiovascular risk into low, moderate, and high categories — but a single value >10 likely reflects acute infection, not chronic vascular inflammation. Always repeat after 2 weeks.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks; urgent if very high with symptoms
high.details: CRP >10 mg/L suggests significant infection or inflammation. CRP 1-3 is intermediate cardiovascular risk. Very high CRP (>100) with fever suggests serious infection needing urgent evaluation.

low.details: Low CRP indicates minimal systemic inflammation. Excellent for cardiovascular risk.

optimal.details: Low CRP (<1 mg/L) indicates low cardiovascular risk. Continue anti-inflammatory diet, exercise, and healthy weight maintenance.

citations.text: Calder PC. Omega-3 fatty acids and inflammatory processes: from molecules to man. Biochem Soc Trans. 2017;45(5):1105-1115.
Ma Y, Griffith JA, Chasan-Taber L, et al. Association between dietary fiber and serum C-reactive protein. Am J Clin Nutr. 2006;83(4):760-766.

Fedewa MV, Hathaway ED, Ward-Ritacco CL. Effect of exercise training on C reactive protein: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised and non-randomised controlled trials. Br J Sports Med. 2017;51(8):670-676.
World Health Organization. C-reactive protein concentrations as a marker of inflammation or infection for interpreting biomarkers of micronutrient status. WHO; 2014.

parameters.name: Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate
parameters.range: Men: 0–15 mm/hr (age <50), 0–20 mm/hr (age >50). Women: 0–20 mm/hr (age <50), 0–30 mm/hr (age >50).

layman.simplyPut: How fast your red blood cells settle in a tube — faster settling suggests inflammation somewhere in your body.
layman.analogy: ESR measures how quickly red blood cells settle to the bottom of a tube — like sand sinking in water. Faster settling suggests inflammation somewhere in your body.
layman.whatItTells: A nonspecific marker of inflammation that measures how quickly red blood cells settle to the bottom of a test tube in one hour.

whenToWorry.worry: Very high ESR (>100) can indicate serious infection, autoimmune disease, or cancer. Needs investigation.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: ESR is a nonspecific test — it tells you something is inflamed but not what. It's elevated in many harmless conditions including pregnancy and aging.

interpretation.high: Moderate elevation (20–50 mm/hr) seen in infection, inflammation, malignancy. High elevation (50–100 mm/hr) in active autoimmune disease, severe infection. Very high (>100 mm/hr) suggests giant cell arteritis, multiple myeloma, or severe infection.
interpretation.low: Low ESR is normal. Very low ESR (<1 mm/hr) may indicate polycythemia, sickle cell disease, or extreme leukocytosis.
interpretation.optimal: Within age and sex-adjusted normal range.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: ESR >100 mm/hr has a limited differential: malignancy (especially multiple myeloma), severe infection, autoimmune disease, and temporal arteritis. It warrants urgent workup — don't dismiss it.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated ESR is a nonspecific inflammation marker. Evaluate for infection, autoimmune disease, malignancy, or chronic inflammatory conditions.

low.details: Low ESR is normal and not clinically significant in most cases.

optimal.details: Normal ESR suggests no significant systemic inflammation.

citations.text: Casas R, Sacanella E, Estruch R. The immune protective effect of the Mediterranean diet against chronic low-grade inflammatory diseases. Endocr Metab Immune Disord Drug Targets. 2014;14(4):245-254.
Kasapis C, Thompson PD. The effects of physical activity on serum C-reactive protein and inflammatory markers: a systematic review. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2005;45(10):1563-1569.

layman.simplyPut: The most common antibody in your blood — protects you from infections you've had before or been vaccinated against.
layman.analogy: IgG is the most abundant antibody in your blood — the experienced soldiers who remember past infections. They're why vaccines work — your IgG remembers and attacks returning invaders.
layman.whatItTells: The most abundant immunoglobulin in serum, comprising 70–80% of total antibodies.

whenToWorry.worry: Low IgG increases infection risk. Very high IgG may indicate chronic infection, autoimmune disease, or multiple myeloma.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: IgG is part of the adaptive immune system. Mildly elevated levels during or after an infection are expected.

interpretation.high: Polyclonal elevation in chronic infections (HIV, hepatitis), autoimmune disease (SLE, RA), and chronic liver disease. Monoclonal elevation (M-spike) suggests multiple myeloma or MGUS.
interpretation.low: Low IgG (<400 mg/dL) indicates humoral immunodeficiency with increased infection susceptibility. Causes include common variable immunodeficiency (CVID), rituximab therapy, protein-losing enteropathy, and nephrotic syndrome.
interpretation.optimal: Middle of reference range (900–1,400 mg/dL) with adequate vaccine responses.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: IgG subclass deficiency (especially IgG2 and IgG4) can cause recurrent sinopulmonary infections despite normal total IgG — check subclasses when clinical suspicion is high and total IgG is normal.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated total IgG may indicate chronic infection, autoimmune disease, liver disease, or monoclonal gammopathy. Serum protein electrophoresis may be needed to characterize.

low.details: Low IgG increases susceptibility to infections, especially bacterial. May indicate primary immunodeficiency, nephrotic syndrome, or medication effects. May need immunoglobulin replacement.

optimal.details: Normal IgG indicates healthy humoral immunity. Continue vaccinations per schedule.

citations.text: Chandra RK. Nutrition and the immune system from birth to old age. Eur J Clin Nutr. 2002;56 Suppl 3:S73-76.
Ross AC. Vitamin A and retinoic acid in T cell-related immunity. Am J Clin Nutr. 2012;96(5):1166S-1172S.
Nieman DC, Wentz LM. The compelling link between physical activity and the body's defense system. J Sport Health Sci. 2019;8(3):201-217.

parameters.name: Immunoglobulin A (Total)
parameters.range: Serum IgA: 70–400 mg/dL (0.7–4.0 g/L). Salivary IgA: 80–350 µg/mL.

layman.simplyPut: Antibodies that guard your gut, lungs, and mucous membranes — your first line of immune defense at body surfaces.
layman.analogy: IgA guards your mucosal surfaces — nose, throat, gut, lungs. Think of it as the bouncer at the door of every entry point to your body.
layman.whatItTells: The predominant antibody in mucosal surfaces (gut, respiratory tract, saliva, tears).

whenToWorry.worry: IgA deficiency (the most common immune deficiency) usually causes no problems but may increase respiratory and GI infections. Very high IgA may indicate IgA nephropathy.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Many people with low IgA are completely healthy. It's the most common primary immune deficiency and usually requires no treatment.

interpretation.high: Elevated in IgA nephropathy, celiac disease (anti-tTG IgA), alcoholic liver disease, chronic infections, and IgA myeloma.
interpretation.low: Selective IgA deficiency usually benign but associated with increased celiac disease, allergies, and autoimmune diseases. Severe IgA deficiency may cause recurrent mucosal infections.
interpretation.optimal: Middle of reference range with adequate mucosal immune function.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Selective IgA deficiency is the most common primary immunodeficiency (1 in 500) — importantly, these patients can have anaphylactic reactions to blood products containing IgA. Always document it prominently.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated IgA may indicate IgA nephropathy, liver disease (cirrhosis), celiac disease, or chronic infections. Check kidney function and consider renal biopsy if hematuria present.

low.details: Selective IgA deficiency (most common primary immunodeficiency) increases risk of mucosal infections, allergies, and autoimmune disease. Important: can cause anaphylaxis with blood transfusions.

optimal.details: Normal IgA supports mucosal immunity in the gut, respiratory tract, and urogenital tract.

citations.text: Yan F, Polk DB. Probiotics and immune health. Curr Opin Gastroenterol. 2011;27(6):496-501.
Cruzat V, Macedo Rogero M, Noel Keane K, et al. Glutamine: Metabolism and immune function, supplementation and clinical translation. Nutrients. 2018;10(11):1564.
Gleeson M, Bishop NC, Oliveira M, Tauler P. Daily probiotic's (Lactobacillus casei Shirota) reduction of infection incidence in athletes. Int J Sport Nutr Exerc Metab. 2011;21(1):55-64.

parameters.name: Total IgE
parameters.range: Total IgE: <100 IU/mL (adults). Varies widely; atopic individuals may have 200–2,000+ IU/mL.

layman.simplyPut: The antibody responsible for allergic reactions — high levels suggest allergies, asthma, or parasitic infections.
layman.analogy: IgE is the allergy antibody — it sits on mast cells like a loaded trigger. When an allergen (pollen, peanuts) binds to IgE, it fires off histamine, causing allergy symptoms.
layman.whatItTells: Immunoglobulin E is the antibody class responsible for allergic reactions and defense against parasitic infections.

whenToWorry.worry: Very high total IgE may indicate severe allergies, asthma, or parasitic infection. Specific IgE tests identify exact allergens.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Having some IgE is normal — everyone has it. The level matters, and many people with mildly elevated IgE have no symptoms.

interpretation.high: Elevated total IgE suggests atopy (asthma, eczema, allergic rhinitis), parasitic infection, allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, hyper-IgE syndrome, or Churg-Strauss syndrome. Does not identify specific allergen.
interpretation.low: Low IgE is common and usually not pathological. May be seen in some immunodeficiency states.
interpretation.optimal: Within reference range without clinical evidence of allergic disease.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Total IgE above 1000 IU/mL with eosinophilia and asthma should prompt evaluation for allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA) — it's underdiagnosed and leads to bronchiectasis if untreated.

high.action: See your doctor or allergist within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated total IgE suggests allergic disease (asthma, eczema, allergic rhinitis), parasitic infection, or rare conditions (allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis, hyperIgE syndrome).

low.details: Low total IgE makes allergic disease less likely, though specific allergen IgE testing is more useful than total IgE for diagnosing allergies.

optimal.details: Normal IgE suggests no significant allergic sensitization. Continue any allergen avoidance measures that work for you.

citations.text: Bozzetto S, Carraro S, Giordano G, et al. Asthma, allergy and respiratory infections: the vitamin D hypothesis. Allergy. 2012;67(1):10-17.
Zuccotti G, Meneghin F, Aceti A, et al. Probiotics for prevention of atopic diseases in infants: systematic review and meta-analysis. Allergy. 2015;70(11):1356-1371.
Eichenberger PA, Diener SN, Kofmehl R, Spengler CM. Effects of exercise training on airway hyperreactivity in asthma: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Sports Med. 2013;43(11):1157-1170.

layman.simplyPut: A vitamin essential for vision, immune function, and skin health — deficiency can cause night blindness.
layman.analogy: Vitamin A is essential for vision, skin, and immune function — it keeps your eyes seeing, your skin healthy, and your immune system armed. Carrots really do help your eyesight.
layman.whatItTells: A fat-soluble vitamin essential for vision, immune function, cell differentiation, and reproduction.

whenToWorry.worry: Deficiency causes night blindness and immune weakness. Toxicity (from excessive supplements, not food) causes liver damage and birth defects.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Getting vitamin A from colorful fruits and vegetables (beta-carotene) is safe — your body only converts what it needs.

interpretation.high: Hypervitaminosis A causes hepatotoxicity, intracranial hypertension, bone pain, and is teratogenic in pregnancy. Chronic intake >10,000 IU/day in adults raises toxicity risk.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes night blindness, xerophthalmia, increased infection susceptibility, and keratinization of epithelial tissues. Most common in developing countries.
interpretation.optimal: 40–60 µg/dL with adequate dietary intake of both preformed retinol and carotenoids.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Beta-carotene (provitamin A) from plants cannot cause toxicity because its conversion is self-regulated — but preformed retinol from supplements and liver absolutely can. This distinction matters for supplement counseling.

high.action: Stop vitamin A supplements; see your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Vitamin A toxicity causes headache, nausea, liver damage, and birth defects in pregnancy. Stop supplements immediately. Chronic toxicity causes bone pain and liver fibrosis.

low.details: Vitamin A deficiency causes night blindness, dry eyes, and impaired immunity. Common in developing countries. Supplement with retinol and increase orange/yellow vegetables.

optimal.details: Adequate vitamin A supports vision, immunity, and skin health. Continue eating colorful fruits and vegetables.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Vitamin A, Vitamin K, Arsenic, Boron, Chromium, Copper, Iodine, Iron, Manganese, Molybdenum, Nickel, Silicon, Vanadium, and Zinc. National Academies Press; 2001.
WHO. Global prevalence of vitamin A deficiency in populations at risk 1995-2005. WHO Global Database on Vitamin A Deficiency. Geneva: WHO; 2009.

parameters.name: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine)
parameters.range: Whole blood thiamine: 70–180 nmol/L. Erythrocyte TPP: 120–150 nmol/L.

layman.simplyPut: Thiamine — powers your brain and nerves by helping convert food into energy; severe deficiency causes beriberi.
layman.analogy: Thiamine helps your body convert food into energy — like the spark plug in your metabolic engine. Without it, your brain and nerves can't function properly.
layman.whatItTells: A water-soluble vitamin essential for carbohydrate metabolism and neural function.

whenToWorry.worry: Severe deficiency causes beriberi (nerve/heart damage) or Wernicke's encephalopathy (brain damage, often from alcoholism).
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Thiamine deficiency is rare in people eating a varied diet. Whole grains, pork, and legumes are good sources.

interpretation.high: No known toxicity from oral thiamine as excess is excreted renally.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes wet beriberi (heart failure, edema), dry beriberi (peripheral neuropathy), or Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion, ataxia, ophthalmoplegia). Most common in alcoholism, bariatric surgery, and hyperemesis gravidarum.
interpretation.optimal: Upper half of reference range with adequate dietary intake from whole grains and fortified foods.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Give thiamine BEFORE glucose in a malnourished or alcoholic patient — glucose infusion without thiamine can precipitate acute Wernicke's encephalopathy by exhausting the last thiamine reserves.

high.action: No action needed — excess is excreted
high.details: Thiamine toxicity from oral supplementation is extremely rare as excess is readily excreted in urine.

low.details: Thiamine deficiency causes Wernicke's encephalopathy (confusion, ataxia, eye movement abnormalities) — a medical emergency. Also causes beriberi (heart failure, neuropathy). Common in alcoholism and malnutrition.

optimal.details: Adequate thiamine supports energy metabolism and nerve function. Found in whole grains, pork, and legumes.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Thiamin, Riboflavin, Niacin, Vitamin B6, Folate, Vitamin B12, Pantothenic Acid, Biotin, and Choline. National Academies Press; 1998.
Woolf K, Manore MM. B-vitamins and exercise: does exercise alter requirements? Int J Sport Nutr Exerc Metab. 2006;16(5):453-484.

parameters.name: Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin)
parameters.range: EGRAC <1.2 (adequate); 1.2–1.4 (marginal); >1.4 (deficient). Serum riboflavin: 6–24 µg/L.

layman.simplyPut: Riboflavin — helps your body produce energy and acts as an antioxidant; deficiency causes cracked lips and light sensitivity.
layman.analogy: Riboflavin helps your cells produce energy and acts as an antioxidant. It's what turns your urine bright yellow after taking a multivitamin — that's excess B2 being excreted.
layman.whatItTells: A water-soluble vitamin that serves as a precursor to the coenzymes flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD).

whenToWorry.worry: Deficiency causes cracked lips, sore throat, and inflamed tongue. Rare in developed countries.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Bright yellow urine after B vitamins is completely harmless — it just means you took more than your body needs right now.

interpretation.high: No known toxicity from excess riboflavin; bright yellow urine is harmless.
interpretation.low: Deficiency (ariboflavinosis) causes angular stomatitis, glossitis, seborrheic dermatitis, and normocytic anemia. Impairs metabolism of B6, niacin, and folate.
interpretation.optimal: EGRAC <1.2 with adequate dietary intake.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Riboflavin makes urine bright fluorescent yellow — this is harmless but alarming to patients who don't expect it. A quick heads-up prevents ER visits.

high.action: No action needed — excess is excreted
high.details: Riboflavin excess is rare and causes bright yellow urine but no toxicity.

low.details: Riboflavin deficiency causes cracked lips (cheilosis), sore throat, and anemia. Often occurs with other B vitamin deficiencies. Supplement and increase dairy, eggs, and leafy greens.

optimal.details: Adequate riboflavin supports energy metabolism and antioxidant defense. Found in dairy, eggs, and lean meats.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Thiamin, Riboflavin, Niacin, Vitamin B6, Folate, Vitamin B12, Pantothenic Acid, Biotin, and Choline. National Academies Press; 1998.
Woolf K, Manore MM. B-vitamins and exercise: does exercise alter requirements? Int J Sport Nutr Exerc Metab. 2006;16(5):453-484.

parameters.name: Vitamin B3 (Niacin)
parameters.range: Urinary N1-methylnicotinamide: 5.8–17.5 µmol/day. No well-established serum reference range.

layman.simplyPut: Niacin — supports energy metabolism and DNA repair; severe deficiency causes pellagra (skin, gut, and brain symptoms).
layman.analogy: Niacin is essential for converting food to energy and repairing DNA. In high doses, it's also used to lower cholesterol — one of the few vitamins that doubles as a medication.
layman.whatItTells: A water-soluble vitamin that is a precursor to NAD+ and NADP+, coenzymes involved in over 400 enzymatic reactions including energy metabolism, DNA repair, and cell signaling.

whenToWorry.worry: Severe deficiency causes pellagra (dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia). High-dose niacin supplements can cause liver damage and flushing.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Pellagra is extremely rare in developed countries. The niacin 'flush' (red, warm skin) from supplements is uncomfortable but harmless.

interpretation.high: Pharmacologic niacin (1–3 g/day) causes flushing, hepatotoxicity, and hyperglycemia. Therapeutic doses used for dyslipidemia raise HDL and lower triglycerides.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes pellagra — the 4 D's: dermatitis, diarrhea, dementia, and death if untreated. Rare in developed countries except in alcoholism and malabsorption.
interpretation.optimal: Adequate dietary intake of 16 mg NE/day for men, 14 mg NE/day for women.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Pellagra's 4 D's: Dermatitis, Diarrhea, Dementia, and Death — it still occurs in alcoholics, those on isoniazid (competes with B3), and in populations dependent on unprocessed corn (low tryptophan bioavailability).

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks if on niacin supplements
high.details: High-dose niacin causes flushing, hepatotoxicity, and worsened glucose control. Sustained-release forms have more liver toxicity risk.

low.details: Severe niacin deficiency causes pellagra (diarrhea, dermatitis, dementia). Seen in alcoholism, malabsorption, and carcinoid syndrome. Supplement with niacinamide.

optimal.details: Adequate niacin supports energy metabolism and DNA repair. Found in poultry, fish, and fortified grains.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Thiamin, Riboflavin, Niacin, Vitamin B6, Folate, Vitamin B12, Pantothenic Acid, Biotin, and Choline. National Academies Press; 1998.
Cantó C, Menzies KJ, Auwerx J. NAD(+) metabolism and the control of energy homeostasis: A balancing act between mitochondria and the nucleus. Cell Metab. 2015;22(1):31-53.

parameters.name: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
parameters.range: Whole blood: 1.6–2.7 µmol/L. Urinary excretion: 2–7 mg/day.

layman.simplyPut: Pantothenic acid — needed to make coenzyme A, which is essential for energy production and hormone synthesis.
layman.analogy: Pantothenic acid is found in almost all foods — 'panto' means everywhere. It helps make coenzyme A, a crucial molecule for energy metabolism.
layman.whatItTells: A water-soluble vitamin that is a component of coenzyme A (CoA) and acyl carrier protein, both essential for fatty acid synthesis and oxidation, citric acid cycle, and the synthesis of steroid hormones, neurotransmitters, and hemoglobin.

whenToWorry.worry: Deficiency is extremely rare due to widespread food sources. Only seen in severe malnutrition.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: You almost certainly get enough B5 from your diet. Supplementation is rarely necessary.

interpretation.high: No known toxicity. Very high doses (10–20 g/day) may cause diarrhea.
interpretation.low: Deficiency is rare; experimentally induced deficiency causes burning feet syndrome (paresthesias), fatigue, insomnia, and GI disturbances.
interpretation.optimal: Adequate intake of 5 mg/day met through a varied diet.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Pantothenic acid is named from the Greek 'pantos' meaning everywhere — deficiency is extraordinarily rare because it's present in virtually all foods. When you see deficiency, look for severe malnutrition or metabolic disorders.

high.action: No action needed — excess is excreted
high.details: Pantothenic acid toxicity is essentially unknown. Very high doses may cause diarrhea.

low.details: Pantothenic acid deficiency is rare but causes fatigue, numbness, and burning feet syndrome. Usually occurs with multiple nutrient deficiencies.

optimal.details: Adequate pantothenic acid supports coenzyme A synthesis and energy metabolism. Widely distributed in foods.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Thiamin, Riboflavin, Niacin, Vitamin B6, Folate, Vitamin B12, Pantothenic Acid, Biotin, and Choline. National Academies Press; 1998.

parameters.name: Vitamin B6 (Pyridoxine)
parameters.range: Plasma PLP: 20–200 nmol/L (5–50 µg/L)

layman.simplyPut: Pyridoxine — critical for brain development, immune function, and making hemoglobin; deficiency causes anemia and nerve problems.
layman.analogy: B6 is involved in over 100 enzyme reactions, especially protein metabolism and brain chemical production. It helps make serotonin and dopamine — your happiness chemicals.
layman.whatItTells: A water-soluble vitamin with six vitamer forms, of which pyridoxal 5'-phosphate (PLP) is the active coenzyme.

whenToWorry.worry: Deficiency causes anemia, confusion, and weakened immunity. Excess from high-dose supplements (>100mg/day) can cause nerve damage.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Most people get adequate B6 from chicken, fish, potatoes, and bananas. Supplementation is only needed with specific deficiencies.

interpretation.high: Chronic high-dose supplementation (>200 mg/day) causes sensory neuropathy. Symptoms include numbness, tingling, and ataxia; usually reversible upon cessation.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes microcytic sideroblastic anemia, peripheral neuropathy, seizures (in infants), depression, confusion, and weakened immunity. Common in elderly and alcoholics.
interpretation.optimal: Plasma PLP 50–100 nmol/L.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Paradoxically, B6 toxicity causes the same peripheral neuropathy as B6 deficiency — this is the most common vitamin toxicity from supplementation. Keep doses under 100 mg/day.

high.action: Stop B6 supplements; see your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Chronic high-dose B6 (>100 mg/day) causes peripheral neuropathy with numbness and tingling. Discontinue supplements and neuropathy usually reverses.

low.details: B6 deficiency causes microcytic anemia, peripheral neuropathy, seizures, and depression. Common with isoniazid use, alcoholism, and malabsorption.

optimal.details: Adequate B6 supports amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis, and immune function.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Thiamin, Riboflavin, Niacin, Vitamin B6, Folate, Vitamin B12, Pantothenic Acid, Biotin, and Choline. National Academies Press; 1998.
Manore MM. Effect of physical activity on thiamine, riboflavin, and vitamin B-6 requirements. Am J Clin Nutr. 2000;72(2 Suppl):598S-606S.

parameters.name: Vitamin B7 (Biotin)
parameters.range: Serum biotin: 200–1,200 pg/mL (0.8–4.9 nmol/L)

layman.simplyPut: Biotin — supports healthy hair, skin, and nails, and helps convert food into energy.
layman.analogy: Biotin supports hair, skin, nails, and metabolism. It's popular in beauty supplements, though evidence for hair growth in non-deficient people is limited.
layman.whatItTells: A water-soluble vitamin that serves as a coenzyme for five carboxylase enzymes involved in gluconeogenesis, fatty acid synthesis, and amino acid metabolism.

whenToWorry.worry: True biotin deficiency (hair loss, rash, neurological symptoms) is rare but can occur with raw egg whites consumption or certain medications.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Biotin supplements can interfere with lab tests (troponin, thyroid, hormones) giving false results. Tell your doctor if you take biotin before blood work.

interpretation.high: Not toxic itself, but high biotin levels interfere with laboratory immunoassays, potentially causing missed diagnoses (e.g., falsely normal troponin in heart attack).
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes dermatitis, alopecia, conjunctivitis, paresthesias, and in infants, seizures. Caused by raw egg white consumption (avidin binds biotin), anticonvulsants, and rare genetic disorders.
interpretation.optimal: Within reference range with adequate intake of 30 µg/day.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Biotin supplementation at doses found in common hair/nail supplements (5-10 mg) causes massive interference with immunoassays — it can produce falsely elevated troponin (missing MI) or falsely low TSH (mimicking Graves'). Stop biotin 48 hours before labs.

high.action: No action needed but inform lab about supplementation
high.details: Biotin supplements (even standard doses) interfere with many lab assays — can falsely elevate troponin, lower TSH, and affect thyroid tests. Stop biotin 48 hours before blood work.

low.details: Biotin deficiency causes hair loss, rash, and neurological symptoms. Rare in adults but seen with raw egg white consumption (avidin binds biotin) and certain medications.

optimal.details: Adequate biotin supports energy metabolism and healthy hair/nails. Found in eggs, nuts, and whole grains.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Thiamin, Riboflavin, Niacin, Vitamin B6, Folate, Vitamin B12, Pantothenic Acid, Biotin, and Choline. National Academies Press; 1998.

parameters.name: Folate (Vitamin B9)
parameters.range: Serum folate: 7–45 nmol/L (3–20 ng/mL). RBC folate: 340–1,020 nmol/L (150–450 ng/mL).

layman.simplyPut: Vitamin B9 — essential for DNA synthesis and preventing birth defects; especially important before and during pregnancy.
layman.analogy: Folate is essential for making new cells and DNA — it's the blueprint copier of your body. Critical during pregnancy because the baby is making billions of new cells.
layman.whatItTells: A water-soluble B vitamin essential for one-carbon metabolism, DNA synthesis, repair, and methylation.

whenToWorry.worry: Low folate causes megaloblastic anemia (large, immature red blood cells). In pregnancy, deficiency causes neural tube defects — supplement before conception.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Folate is added to many foods (fortified bread, cereal). Most people in developed countries get adequate amounts.

interpretation.high: Excess folate may mask vitamin B12 deficiency by correcting anemia while neurological damage progresses. Very high folic acid (not 5-MTHF) intake may promote existing tumors through enhanced DNA synthesis.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia (macrocytic with hypersegmented neutrophils), elevated homocysteine, and neural tube defects (spina bifida, anencephaly) in pregnancy.
interpretation.optimal: RBC folate >600 nmol/L. Women of childbearing age should have >906 nmol/L (400 ng/mL) RBC folate for NTD prevention.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: In the era of folate fortification, frank folate deficiency is rare in developed countries — when you see macrocytic anemia, B12 deficiency is now far more likely than folate deficiency as the cause.

high.action: Reduce folic acid supplementation
high.details: High folate is generally safe but can mask B12 deficiency by correcting anemia without treating neurological damage. Always check B12 when folate is high.

low.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks; urgent if pregnant or planning pregnancy
low.details: Folate deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and neural tube defects in pregnancy. Supplement 400 mcg/day (800 mcg if pregnant). Increase leafy greens and fortified grains.

optimal.details: Adequate folate supports DNA synthesis, red blood cell production, and fetal development. Essential for women of childbearing age.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Thiamin, Riboflavin, Niacin, Vitamin B6, Folate, Vitamin B12, Pantothenic Acid, Biotin, and Choline. National Academies Press; 1998.
MRC Vitamin Study Research Group. Prevention of neural tube defects: results of the Medical Research Council Vitamin Study. Lancet. 1991;338(8760):131-137.
World Health Organization. Serum and red blood cell folate concentrations for assessing folate status in populations. WHO; 2015.

parameters.name: Vitamin B12 (Cobalamin)
parameters.range: Serum B12: 200–900 pg/mL (148–664 pmol/L). MMA: <370 nmol/L.

layman.simplyPut: Cobalamin — vital for nerves, red blood cells, and DNA; deficiency causes fatigue, nerve damage, and cognitive issues.
layman.analogy: B12 is essential for nerve function and red blood cell production. Your body stores years' worth in the liver, so deficiency develops slowly — like a slow leak that takes months to notice.
layman.whatItTells: A water-soluble vitamin essential for DNA synthesis, methylation, neurological function, and red blood cell formation.

whenToWorry.worry: B12 below 200 pg/mL can cause irreversible nerve damage if untreated. Symptoms include numbness, tingling, balance problems, and cognitive changes.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: B12 deficiency is easily treated with supplements or injections. Vegans and older adults should monitor B12 regularly.

interpretation.high: Very high B12 (>1,000 pg/mL without supplementation) may indicate liver disease, myeloproliferative neoplasms, or renal failure. No toxicity from oral supplementation.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia, subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord, peripheral neuropathy, cognitive impairment, and glossitis. Neurological damage can be irreversible.
interpretation.optimal: Serum B12 >400 pg/mL with normal MMA and homocysteine.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: B12 deficiency can cause irreversible subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord even with a normal CBC — neurological symptoms can appear before anemia. Check methylmalonic acid if B12 is borderline (200-400 pg/mL).

high.action: Discuss at next appointment
high.details: Very high B12 levels may indicate liver disease, myeloproliferative disorders, or excessive supplementation. Usually not harmful from supplements.

low.details: B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and irreversible neurological damage (subacute combined degeneration) if untreated. Common in vegans, elderly, and those on metformin or PPIs.

optimal.details: Normal B12 supports nerve function, DNA synthesis, and red blood cell production. Vegans should supplement.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Thiamin, Riboflavin, Niacin, Vitamin B6, Folate, Vitamin B12, Pantothenic Acid, Biotin, and Choline. National Academies Press; 1998.
Green R, Allen LH, Bjørke-Monsen AL, et al. Vitamin B12 deficiency. Nat Rev Dis Primers. 2017;3:17040.
World Health Organization. Serum cobalamin concentrations for assessing vitamin B12 status in populations. WHO; 2023.

parameters.name: Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid)
parameters.range: Plasma: 0.6–2.0 mg/dL (34–114 µmol/L). Deficiency: <0.2 mg/dL (<11 µmol/L).

layman.simplyPut: Ascorbic acid — a powerful antioxidant that supports immune function, wound healing, and iron absorption.
layman.analogy: Vitamin C is your body's repair crew and antioxidant shield — it helps heal wounds, builds collagen, and protects cells from damage. Sailors got scurvy without it.
layman.whatItTells: A water-soluble vitamin and potent antioxidant essential for collagen synthesis, carnitine biosynthesis, neurotransmitter production, and immune function.

whenToWorry.worry: Scurvy (bleeding gums, poor wound healing, fatigue) from severe deficiency is rare but still occurs in people with very poor diets.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: You can't really overdose on vitamin C from food. Excess is simply excreted in urine. Mega-doses from supplements may cause kidney stones in susceptible people.

interpretation.high: Generally safe; excess excreted renally. Very high doses (>2 g/day) may cause diarrhea and increase oxalate kidney stone risk in susceptible individuals.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes scurvy — fatigue, gum bleeding, petechiae, impaired wound healing, corkscrew hairs, and eventually death. Subclinical deficiency impairs immunity and collagen synthesis.
interpretation.optimal: Plasma >0.7 mg/dL (>40 µmol/L). Saturating intake ~200 mg/day from food.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Scurvy is not just a historical disease — it still occurs in elderly patients living alone, smokers, and people who eat no fruits or vegetables. Think of it when you see poor wound healing, perifollicular hemorrhages, and corkscrew hairs.

high.action: Reduce supplementation to avoid kidney stones
high.details: Very high vitamin C (>2000 mg/day) increases oxalate kidney stone risk and may cause GI upset. Excess is excreted but renal stones are a real risk.

low.details: Vitamin C deficiency causes scurvy — bleeding gums, poor wound healing, fatigue, and joint pain. Increase citrus fruits, berries, and bell peppers.

optimal.details: Adequate vitamin C supports collagen synthesis, immune function, and antioxidant defense.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Vitamin C, Vitamin E, Selenium, and Carotenoids. National Academies Press; 2000.
Hemilä H, Chalker E. Vitamin C for preventing and treating the common cold. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2013;(1):CD000980.

parameters.name: Vitamin D (25-Hydroxyvitamin D)
parameters.range: Sufficient: 30–100 ng/mL (75–250 nmol/L). Insufficient: 20–29 ng/mL. Deficient: <20 ng/mL (<50 nmol/L).

layman.simplyPut: The 'sunshine vitamin' — essential for strong bones, immune defense, and mood; deficiency is very common worldwide.
layman.analogy: Vitamin D is the 'sunshine vitamin' — your skin makes it from sunlight. It's actually a hormone that affects bone health, immunity, mood, and hundreds of genes.
layman.whatItTells: A fat-soluble prohormone essential for calcium absorption, bone mineralization, immune modulation, and muscle function.

whenToWorry.worry: Below 20 ng/mL is deficient. Below 10 causes severe bone weakness. Deficiency is linked to increased infection risk, depression, and autoimmune disease.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Vitamin D deficiency is the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide. It's easily correctable with supplements (1000-4000 IU/day for most adults).

interpretation.high: Toxicity rare but possible at >150 ng/mL — causes hypercalcemia, renal damage, and soft tissue calcification. Usually from excessive supplementation (>10,000 IU/day chronically).
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults), increased fracture risk, muscle weakness, and is associated with autoimmune diseases, depression, cardiovascular disease, and cancer.
interpretation.optimal: 40–60 ng/mL (100–150 nmol/L). Levels associated with lowest all-cause mortality.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Vitamin D is actually a hormone, not a vitamin — it's synthesized in skin, hydroxylated in liver and kidney, and acts on nuclear receptors throughout the body. This explains its pleiotropic effects beyond bone.

high.action: Stop vitamin D supplements; see your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Vitamin D >100 ng/mL causes hypercalcemia (nausea, confusion, kidney stones, cardiac arrhythmias). Stop supplements and check calcium. Sun exposure alone cannot cause toxicity.

low.details: Vitamin D <20 ng/mL is deficient, increasing risks of osteoporosis, fractures, immune dysfunction, and depression. Supplement 1000-4000 IU/day depending on severity.

optimal.details: Adequate vitamin D (30-50 ng/mL) supports bone health, immunity, and mood. Get 15-20 min of midday sun or supplement.

citations.text: Holick MF, Binkley NC, Bischoff-Ferrari HA, et al. Evaluation, treatment, and prevention of vitamin D deficiency: an Endocrine Society clinical practice guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab. 2011;96(7):1911-1930.
Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Calcium and Vitamin D. National Academies Press; 2011.
World Health Organization. Nutritional rickets: a review of disease burden, causes, diagnosis, prevention and treatment. WHO; 2019.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Low 25-OH-D + secondary hyperparathyroidism + osteomalacia
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: 25-OH-D < 20 ng/mL deficient; 20-30 insufficient; check PTH, calcium, phosphorus, ALP

drugInteractions.mechanism: Bile acid sequestrant impairs fat-soluble vitamin absorption
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Separate vitamin D supplementation by 4h; monitor 25-OH-D levels

labMethodology.stability: Stable 7 days refrigerated; relatively robust analyte

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Target >30 ng/mL. Sun exposure 10-15 min/day adequate for light-skinned individuals in summer.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Skin vitamin D production declines ~50% compared to age 20. Supplementation increasingly necessary.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Most adults need 1,000-2,000 IU/day. Higher doses for those with obesity or malabsorption.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Elderly need 800-2,000 IU/day minimum. Vitamin D deficiency is nearly universal without supplementation. Reduces fall risk by 20%.

globalRanges.who: Sufficient ≥20 ng/mL (50 nmol/L); some experts recommend ≥30 ng/mL (75 nmol/L)
globalRanges.african: Lower 25-OH-D levels but may have adequate free/bioavailable vitamin D due to lower vitamin D binding protein
globalRanges.asian: Very high prevalence of deficiency in South and East Asia (50-90%); cultural, dietary, and genetic factors
globalRanges.highAltitude: Greater UV exposure can increase synthesis despite cold; but clothing coverage may offset
globalRanges.pediatric: Rickets prevention: 400 IU/day from birth per AAP; higher doses in deficient populations
globalRanges.pregnancy: Deficiency linked to preeclampsia, gestational diabetes, SGA; 600-4000 IU/day recommended depending on baseline

parameters.name: Vitamin E (Alpha-Tocopherol)
parameters.range: 5–20 mg/L (12–46 µmol/L). Lipid-adjusted ratio: >0.8 mg/g total lipids.

layman.simplyPut: A fat-soluble antioxidant that protects cell membranes from damage — supports skin health and immune function.
layman.analogy: Vitamin E is a powerful antioxidant that protects your cell membranes — like a bodyguard that shields your cells from free radical damage.
layman.whatItTells: A fat-soluble antioxidant that protects cell membranes from lipid peroxidation.

whenToWorry.worry: Deficiency (rare) causes nerve damage and muscle weakness. High-dose supplements (>400 IU/day) may increase bleeding risk and mortality.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Most people get enough from nuts, seeds, and vegetable oils. Supplementation is rarely needed and may be harmful in excess.

interpretation.high: Supplementation >400 IU/day may increase all-cause mortality and hemorrhagic stroke risk (antagonizes vitamin K-dependent clotting). Safe UL: 1,000 mg/day.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes peripheral neuropathy, ataxia, retinopathy, and hemolytic anemia. Most commonly seen in fat malabsorption syndromes.
interpretation.optimal: 8–15 mg/L from dietary sources (nuts, seeds, oils) rather than supplements.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: High-dose vitamin E supplementation (>400 IU/day) was associated with increased all-cause mortality in meta-analyses — more is not better. This ended the era of megadose antioxidant supplementation.

high.action: Stop vitamin E supplements; see your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: High-dose vitamin E (>400 IU/day) may increase bleeding risk and all-cause mortality. Discontinue supplements, especially if on anticoagulants.

low.details: Vitamin E deficiency causes peripheral neuropathy and ataxia, usually from fat malabsorption (cystic fibrosis, celiac, short bowel). Supplement with fat-soluble E.

optimal.details: Adequate vitamin E supports antioxidant defense and immune function. Found in nuts, seeds, and vegetable oils.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Vitamin C, Vitamin E, Selenium, and Carotenoids. National Academies Press; 2000.
Miller ER 3rd, Pastor-Barriuso R, Dalal D, et al. Meta-analysis: high-dosage vitamin E supplementation may increase all-cause mortality. Ann Intern Med. 2005;142(1):37-46.

parameters.name: Vitamin K
parameters.range: Serum phylloquinone: 0.2–3.2 nmol/L (0.1–1.4 ng/mL). PT/INR normal: 11–13.5 sec / 0.9–1.1.

layman.simplyPut: Essential for blood clotting and bone health — deficiency causes excessive bleeding; important for newborns.
layman.analogy: Vitamin K is the clotting vitamin — it helps your blood form clots to stop bleeding. Without it, even a paper cut would bleed for hours. It also helps calcium go to bones, not arteries.
layman.whatItTells: A fat-soluble vitamin essential for blood coagulation and bone metabolism.

whenToWorry.worry: Deficiency causes easy bruising and excessive bleeding. If on blood thinners (warfarin), vitamin K intake must stay consistent.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Your gut bacteria make some vitamin K, and leafy greens provide the rest. Deficiency is uncommon in adults eating varied diets.

interpretation.high: No known toxicity from natural vitamin K (K1 or K2). Menadione (synthetic K3) can cause toxicity. High K intake interferes with warfarin therapy.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes coagulopathy (elevated PT/INR, bleeding). Subclinical deficiency promotes vascular calcification (K2) and osteoporosis. Risk factors: malabsorption, antibiotics, liver disease.
interpretation.optimal: Adequate intake (90–120 µg/day K1) with consideration of K2 supplementation for bone and cardiovascular health.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Patients on warfarin should NOT avoid vitamin K — they should eat a CONSISTENT amount. Wild fluctuations in green vegetable intake cause unstable INR, not the vegetables themselves.

high.action: No action needed unless on warfarin
high.details: Vitamin K excess is not toxic from food sources. However, if on warfarin, maintain CONSISTENT vitamin K intake (don't eliminate, just keep steady) to avoid INR fluctuations.

low.action: See a doctor within 1-3 days if bleeding
low.details: Vitamin K deficiency causes bleeding (bruising, gum bleeding, heavy periods). Check PT/INR. Common in newborns, malabsorption, and prolonged antibiotics. Supplement with phytonadione.

optimal.details: Adequate vitamin K supports blood clotting and bone metabolism. Found in leafy greens, broccoli, and fermented foods.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Vitamin A, Vitamin K, Arsenic, Boron, Chromium, Copper, Iodine, Iron, Manganese, Molybdenum, Nickel, Silicon, Vanadium, and Zinc. National Academies Press; 2001.
Geleijnse JM, Vermeer C, Grobbee DE, et al. Dietary intake of menaquinone is associated with a reduced risk of coronary heart disease: the Rotterdam Study. J Nutr. 2004;134(11):3100-3105.

Knapen MH, Drummen NE, Smit E, Vermeer C, Theuwissen E. Three-year low-dose menaquinone-7 supplementation helps decrease bone loss in healthy postmenopausal women. Osteoporos Int. 2013;24(9):2499-2507.
World Health Organization. Vitamin K for the prevention of bleeding in newborns. WHO; 2022.

parameters.name: Ferritin
parameters.range: Men: 30–300 ng/mL. Women (premenopausal): 10–200 ng/mL. Women (postmenopausal): 30–300 ng/mL.

layman.simplyPut: Your body's iron reserve — low ferritin is the earliest sign of iron deficiency, before anemia develops.
layman.analogy: Iron-ferritin is your body's iron savings account — it measures stored iron reserves. Like checking your bank balance rather than your daily spending.
layman.whatItTells: The primary intracellular iron storage protein.

whenToWorry.worry: Ferritin below 30 usually means iron deficiency. Above 1000 may indicate iron overload (hemochromatosis) or severe inflammation.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Ferritin is an acute phase reactant — it rises during any illness or inflammation. One high reading during a cold doesn't mean iron overload.

interpretation.high: Elevated ferritin (>500 ng/mL) may indicate hereditary hemochromatosis, alcoholic liver disease, NASH, infection/inflammation, or malignancy. Very high (>1,000 ng/mL) increases organ damage risk.
interpretation.low: Low ferritin (<30 ng/mL) indicates iron deficiency, the most common nutritional deficiency worldwide. Causes fatigue, hair loss, restless legs, pica, and eventually iron-deficiency anemia.
interpretation.optimal: 50–150 ng/mL for most adults. Female athletes should maintain >40 ng/mL for performance.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: In hospitalized patients, ferritin <30 confirms iron deficiency regardless of inflammation, but ferritin 30-100 is a gray zone where transferrin saturation <20% and CRP levels help distinguish true deficiency from anemia of chronic disease.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated ferritin may indicate iron overload (hemochromatosis), inflammation, liver disease, or malignancy. Check transferrin saturation and CRP to differentiate.

low.details: Low ferritin is the earliest and most specific marker of iron deficiency. Increase iron-rich foods, take iron supplements with vitamin C, and identify cause of depletion.

optimal.details: Normal ferritin indicates adequate iron stores. Continue balanced diet with heme iron sources and vitamin C for absorption.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Vitamin A, Vitamin K, Arsenic, Boron, Chromium, Copper, Iodine, Iron, Manganese, Molybdenum, Nickel, Silicon, Vanadium, and Zinc. National Academies Press; 2001.
Peeling P, Dawson B, Goodman C, et al. Athletic induced iron deficiency: new insights into the role of inflammation, cytokines and hormones. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2008;103(4):381-391.
World Health Organization. WHO guideline on use of ferritin concentrations to assess iron status in individuals and populations. WHO; 2020.

layman.simplyPut: How much room your blood has to carry more iron — high values usually mean your body needs more iron.
layman.analogy: TIBC measures how many empty seats are on the iron-carrying taxis in your blood. High TIBC means lots of empty seats — your body is hungry for iron.
layman.whatItTells: A measure of the blood's capacity to bind iron with transferrin, the primary iron transport protein.

whenToWorry.worry: High TIBC with low iron and ferritin confirms iron deficiency anemia. Low TIBC may indicate chronic disease or iron overload.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: TIBC is a supporting test best interpreted alongside iron, ferritin, and transferrin saturation — not alone.

interpretation.high: Elevated TIBC (>400 µg/dL) indicates iron deficiency — the liver produces more transferrin to maximize iron capture. Also elevated in pregnancy and oral contraceptive use.
interpretation.low: Low TIBC (<250 µg/dL) suggests iron overload (hemochromatosis), chronic inflammatory disease, liver disease, or malnutrition.
interpretation.optimal: Within reference range with transferrin saturation 20–50%.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: TIBC rises in iron deficiency (body wants more iron-carrying capacity) and falls in chronic disease (body actively withholds iron from pathogens) — this pattern helps distinguish the two most common causes of anemia.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Elevated TIBC indicates iron deficiency — the body is making more transferrin to capture scarce iron. Evaluate diet, check for blood loss, and supplement iron.

low.details: Low TIBC may indicate iron overload, chronic inflammation, liver disease, or malnutrition. Further workup needed.

optimal.details: Normal TIBC reflects adequate iron stores and balanced iron metabolism.

citations.text: Camaschella C. Iron-deficiency anemia. N Engl J Med. 2015;372(19):1832-1843.
Peeling P, Dawson B, Goodman C, et al. Athletic induced iron deficiency: new insights into the role of inflammation, cytokines and hormones. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2008;103(4):381-391.

layman.simplyPut: What percentage of your iron-transport protein is actually carrying iron — helps diagnose iron problems.
layman.analogy: Transferrin is the taxi that carries iron through your blood. Saturation tells you what percentage of taxis are full — too few loaded means iron deficiency, too many means iron overload.
layman.whatItTells: The percentage of transferrin binding sites occupied by iron, calculated as (serum iron / TIBC) × 100.

whenToWorry.worry: Below 20% suggests iron deficiency. Above 45% may indicate hemochromatosis (iron overload disorder).
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Transferrin saturation varies with meals and time of day. Morning fasting values are most reliable.

interpretation.high: TSAT >45%: screen for hereditary hemochromatosis (HFE gene test). TSAT >60%: significant iron overload with risk of organ damage. Iron begins depositing in liver, heart, pancreas, and joints.
interpretation.low: TSAT <16%: iron-deficient erythropoiesis (insufficient iron supply to bone marrow). Combined with low ferritin: iron-deficiency anemia. With normal/high ferritin: anemia of chronic disease.
interpretation.optimal: 25–35% — adequate iron supply without excess.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Transferrin saturation >45% on two occasions should prompt HFE gene testing for hereditary hemochromatosis — catching it early prevents irreversible liver cirrhosis and cardiomyopathy.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: TSAT >45% suggests iron overload — screen for hereditary hemochromatosis with HFE gene testing. Phlebotomy may be needed.

low.details: Low TSAT (<20%) confirms iron deficiency, even if ferritin is normal (ferritin is falsely elevated by inflammation). Start iron supplementation.

optimal.details: Normal transferrin saturation indicates good iron balance between intake and utilization.

citations.text: Adams PC, Barton JC. A diagnostic approach to hyperferritinemia with a non-elevated transferrin saturation. J Hepatol. 2011;55(2):453-458.
Peeling P, Dawson B, Goodman C, et al. Athletic induced iron deficiency. Eur J Appl Physiol. 2008;103(4):381-391.

layman.simplyPut: A mineral essential for immune function, wound healing, and taste — even mild deficiency weakens your immunity.
layman.analogy: Zinc is an essential mineral involved in immune function, wound healing, and taste/smell. It's like a Swiss Army knife — small but used for hundreds of different tasks in your body.
layman.whatItTells: An essential trace mineral involved in over 300 enzymatic reactions, including DNA synthesis, immune function, wound healing, protein synthesis, and taste/smell perception.

whenToWorry.worry: Low zinc causes poor wound healing, hair loss, decreased taste/smell, and frequent infections. Common in elderly and vegetarians.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Mild zinc deficiency is common but easily correctable with diet (oysters, beef, beans) or low-dose supplements.

interpretation.high: Zinc toxicity (>150 µg/dL) from excessive supplementation causes copper deficiency, anemia, and immune suppression. Chronic intake >40 mg/day inhibits copper absorption.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes immune dysfunction, dermatitis, alopecia, diarrhea, delayed wound healing, hypogeusia (taste loss), and growth retardation in children. Affects 2 billion people globally.
interpretation.optimal: 80–110 µg/dL with adequate dietary intake.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Zinc supplementation reduces the duration of the common cold by 1-2 days IF started within 24 hours of symptom onset — but the lozenges must dissolve slowly in the mouth (ionic zinc contact with pharyngeal mucosa).

high.action: Stop zinc supplements; see your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Chronic zinc excess causes copper deficiency (leading to anemia and neuropathy), nausea, and immune suppression. Stop supplements exceeding 40 mg/day.

low.details: Zinc deficiency impairs immune function, wound healing, taste/smell, and growth. Common in vegetarians, elderly, and those with GI disease. Supplement 15-30 mg/day.

optimal.details: Adequate zinc supports immune function, wound healing, and protein synthesis. Found in meat, shellfish, legumes, and seeds.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Vitamin A, Vitamin K, Arsenic, Boron, Chromium, Copper, Iodine, Iron, Manganese, Molybdenum, Nickel, Silicon, Vanadium, and Zinc. National Academies Press; 2001.
Micheletti A, Rossi R, Rufini S. Zinc status in athletes: relation to diet and exercise. Sports Med. 2001;31(8):577-582.
World Health Organization. Zinc supplementation in the management of diarrhoea. WHO; 2023.

layman.simplyPut: A trace mineral that protects cells from damage and supports thyroid function — too much is toxic.
layman.analogy: Selenium is a trace mineral that powers your thyroid and antioxidant defenses. Just a tiny amount is needed — like a catalyst in a chemical reaction.
layman.whatItTells: An essential trace mineral incorporated as selenocysteine into 25 selenoproteins including glutathione peroxidases (antioxidant defense), thioredoxin reductases, and iodothyronine deiodinases (thyroid hormone conversion).

whenToWorry.worry: Deficiency can cause thyroid problems and weakened immunity. Excess (>400 µg/day from supplements) causes selenosis — hair loss, nail brittleness, garlic breath.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: One or two Brazil nuts daily provides all the selenium you need. Most people on a varied diet get enough.

interpretation.high: Selenosis occurs at >400 µg/day: garlic breath, hair loss, nail changes, nausea, and peripheral neuropathy. Excess selenium may increase type 2 diabetes risk.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes Keshan disease (cardiomyopathy), Kashin-Beck disease (osteoarthropathy), hypothyroidism, impaired immunity, and increased cancer susceptibility. Rare in developed countries.
interpretation.optimal: 100–130 µg/L. Sufficient to maximize selenoprotein P and glutathione peroxidase activity.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Just 1-2 Brazil nuts per day provides more than the daily selenium requirement — this is the most cost-effective and natural way to supplement selenium, but varies by soil content in growing region.

high.action: Stop selenium supplements; see your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Selenium toxicity (selenosis) causes garlic breath, hair loss, nail brittleness, and neuropathy. Upper limit is 400 mcg/day. Stop supplementation.

low.details: Selenium deficiency impairs thyroid function, immune response, and antioxidant defense. Associated with Keshan disease (cardiomyopathy). Supplement 55-200 mcg/day.

optimal.details: Adequate selenium supports thyroid function and antioxidant defense. Just 1-2 Brazil nuts/day provides sufficient selenium.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Vitamin C, Vitamin E, Selenium, and Carotenoids. National Academies Press; 2000.
Rayman MP. Selenium and human health. Lancet. 2012;379(9822):1256-1268.
World Health Organization. Trace elements in human nutrition and health. WHO; 1996.

parameters.name: Copper
parameters.range: Serum copper: 70–140 µg/dL (11–22 µmol/L). Ceruloplasmin: 20–35 mg/dL.

layman.simplyPut: A trace mineral needed for iron metabolism, nerve function, and making connective tissue.
layman.analogy: Copper helps your body make red blood cells, maintain nerve cells, and strengthen your immune system. It also helps your body absorb iron — they work as a team.
layman.whatItTells: An essential trace mineral required for iron metabolism (ceruloplasmin), connective tissue formation (lysyl oxidase), energy production (cytochrome c oxidase), antioxidant defense (Cu/Zn-SOD), and neurotransmitter synthesis (dopamine beta-hydroxylase).

whenToWorry.worry: Deficiency causes anemia and nerve damage (mimics B12 deficiency). Excess (Wilson's disease) damages liver and brain.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Copper deficiency from diet alone is rare. It's most common after gastric bypass surgery or excessive zinc supplementation.

interpretation.high: Elevated free copper (Wilson disease) causes hepatotoxicity, neuropsychiatric symptoms, and Kayser-Fleischer rings. Acute copper toxicity causes GI distress, hemolysis, and organ damage.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes microcytic or normocytic anemia (iron-refractory), neutropenia, osteoporosis, and myeloneuropathy. Often caused by excess zinc supplementation, gastric bypass, or Menkes disease.
interpretation.optimal: 90–120 µg/dL with normal ceruloplasmin and no excess free copper.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Copper deficiency is the great mimicker of B12 deficiency — both cause myelopathy, neuropathy, and cytopenias. The most common cause in modern medicine is zinc oversupplementation (zinc induces metallothionein that blocks copper absorption).

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Copper excess may indicate Wilson's disease (genetic copper overload causing liver and brain damage), contaminated water, or supplement toxicity. Check ceruloplasmin.

low.details: Copper deficiency causes anemia (refractory to iron), neutropenia, and myelopathy mimicking B12 deficiency. Often from excess zinc supplementation or bariatric surgery.

optimal.details: Adequate copper supports iron metabolism, connective tissue, and neurological function. Found in shellfish, nuts, and organ meats.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Vitamin A, Vitamin K, Arsenic, Boron, Chromium, Copper, Iodine, Iron, Manganese, Molybdenum, Nickel, Silicon, Vanadium, and Zinc. National Academies Press; 2001.
Gaetke LM, Chow-Johnson HS, Chow CK. Copper: toxicological relevance and mechanisms. Arch Toxicol. 2014;88(11):1929-1938.
World Health Organization. Trace elements in human nutrition and health. WHO; 1996.

parameters.name: Iodine
parameters.range: Median UIC: 100–199 µg/L (adequate). 50–99 µg/L (mild deficiency). <50 µg/L (moderate deficiency). <20 µg/L (severe).

layman.simplyPut: A mineral your thyroid needs to make hormones — deficiency is the world's leading cause of preventable brain damage.
layman.analogy: Iodine is the key ingredient your thyroid needs to make thyroid hormones — without it, your thyroid can't manufacture its products, like a factory without raw materials.
layman.whatItTells: An essential trace mineral required exclusively for thyroid hormone synthesis (T3 and T4).

whenToWorry.worry: Deficiency causes goiter (enlarged thyroid) and hypothyroidism. In pregnancy, severe deficiency causes intellectual disability in the baby.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Most table salt is iodized, so deficiency is uncommon in developed countries. Seafood and dairy are also good sources.

interpretation.high: Excessive iodine (UIC >300 µg/L) can cause thyroiditis, hyperthyroidism (Jod-Basedow), or hypothyroidism (Wolff-Chaikoff effect) in susceptible individuals.
interpretation.low: Deficiency causes goiter, hypothyroidism, cretinism (in utero), intellectual disability, and reproductive failure. Even mild maternal deficiency reduces child IQ by 8–13 points.
interpretation.optimal: Median UIC 100–199 µg/L for population; individual assessment through thyroid function tests.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Iodine deficiency remains the most common preventable cause of intellectual disability worldwide — iodized salt has been one of the most successful public health interventions in history.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Excess iodine can cause both hyperthyroidism and hypothyroidism (Wolff-Chaikoff effect). Common from supplements, contrast dye, or amiodarone. Check thyroid function.

low.details: Iodine deficiency causes goiter, hypothyroidism, and cretinism in newborns. Use iodized salt and increase seafood. Most common preventable cause of intellectual disability worldwide.
