optimal.details: Adequate iodine supports thyroid hormone synthesis. Iodized salt and seafood provide sufficient amounts for most people.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Vitamin A, Vitamin K, Arsenic, Boron, Chromium, Copper, Iodine, Iron, Manganese, Molybdenum, Nickel, Silicon, Vanadium, and Zinc. National Academies Press; 2001.
WHO. Assessment of iodine deficiency disorders and monitoring their elimination: a guide for programme managers. 3rd ed. Geneva: WHO; 2007.

parameters.name: RBC Magnesium
parameters.range: RBC Mg: 4.2–6.8 mg/dL (1.7–2.8 mmol/L). Compare with serum Mg: 1.7–2.2 mg/dL.

layman.simplyPut: Magnesium measured inside red blood cells — a more accurate reflection of your body's true magnesium status.
layman.analogy: RBC magnesium measures magnesium inside your red blood cells — a more accurate picture of your body's true magnesium status than the standard blood test, which only measures what's floating in plasma.
layman.whatItTells: Intracellular magnesium status measured in red blood cells, which better reflects tissue magnesium status than serum levels.

whenToWorry.worry: Low RBC magnesium with muscle cramps, fatigue, or heart rhythm issues suggests true magnesium deficiency.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: This is a specialized test. Regular serum magnesium is usually sufficient for routine monitoring.

interpretation.high: Rarely clinically significant. Very high RBC magnesium is uncommon.
interpretation.low: Low RBC magnesium (even with normal serum Mg) associated with muscle cramps, fatigue, insomnia, anxiety, migraine, insulin resistance, hypertension, arrhythmias, and osteoporosis. Subclinical magnesium deficiency is widespread.
interpretation.optimal: Upper half of reference range (5.5–6.8 mg/dL).

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Only 0.3% of total body magnesium is in serum — RBC magnesium better reflects intracellular stores. A patient can have severe magnesium depletion with a normal serum level, which is why empiric supplementation based on symptoms is often appropriate.

high.action: No action needed
high.details: Elevated RBC magnesium is rare and not clinically significant. May reflect recent supplementation.

low.details: Low RBC magnesium is a better indicator of true deficiency than serum magnesium. Supplement with magnesium glycinate or citrate. Increase nuts, seeds, and leafy greens.

optimal.details: Normal RBC magnesium confirms adequate intracellular magnesium stores supporting hundreds of enzymatic reactions.

citations.text: DiNicolantonio JJ, O'Keefe JH, Wilson W. Subclinical magnesium deficiency: a principal driver of cardiovascular disease and a public health crisis. Open Heart. 2018;5(1):e000668.
Nielsen FH, Lukaski HC. Update on the relationship between magnesium and exercise. Magnes Res. 2006;19(3):180-189.

parameters.name: Omega-3 Index
parameters.range: Desirable: ≥8%. Intermediate: 4–8%. High risk: <4%.

layman.simplyPut: The percentage of omega-3 fats in your red blood cell membranes — above 8% is heart-protective.
layman.analogy: The omega-3 index measures the percentage of omega-3 fatty acids in your red blood cell membranes — like checking what type of oil your engine is running on. Higher omega-3s mean less inflammation.
layman.whatItTells: The percentage of EPA (eicosapentaenoic acid) plus DHA (docosahexaenoic acid) in red blood cell membranes.

whenToWorry.worry: An omega-3 index below 4% is associated with significantly higher cardiovascular risk. Target is above 8%.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: You can improve your omega-3 index with 2+ servings of fatty fish per week or fish oil supplements. Changes take 2-3 months.

interpretation.high: Omega-3 Index ≥8% associated with 90% lower risk of sudden cardiac death compared to <4%. Levels up to 12–14% are safe and observed in populations with high fish intake.
interpretation.low: Omega-3 Index <4% is associated with highest risk of cardiovascular death, cognitive decline, and depression. The greatest risk factor for sudden cardiac death.
interpretation.optimal: 8–12%. Associated with lowest cardiovascular risk and best cognitive function.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: The omega-3 index is the RBC membrane content of EPA+DHA — it reflects 120-day intake (like HbA1c for omega-3s). An index >8% is associated with 90% lower sudden cardiac death risk compared to <4%.

high.action: No action needed — excellent for heart and brain
high.details: High omega-3 index (>8%) is cardioprotective and supports brain health. Continue your current diet and supplementation.

low.action: Increase omega-3 intake; discuss supplements with doctor
low.details: Low omega-3 index (<4%) is associated with increased cardiovascular mortality. Eat fatty fish 2-3 times/week or supplement with 1-2 g EPA+DHA daily.

optimal.details: Optimal omega-3 index (8-12%) reduces cardiovascular risk by up to 30%. Continue eating fatty fish and/or supplementing.

citations.text: Harris WS, Von Schacky C. The Omega-3 Index: a new risk factor for death from coronary heart disease? Prev Med. 2004;39(1):212-220.
Philpott JD, Witard OC, Galloway SDR. Applications of omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acid supplementation for sport performance. Res Sports Med. 2019;27(2):219-237.

layman.simplyPut: A protein that reflects your recent nutritional intake — drops faster than albumin when nutrition is poor.
layman.analogy: Prealbumin is a short-lived protein that reflects your nutritional status over the past 2-3 days — like checking your daily food intake rather than your monthly average.
layman.whatItTells: A hepatic transport protein with a short half-life (2–3 days) that makes it a sensitive marker of recent nutritional status and protein-calorie intake.

whenToWorry.worry: Low prealbumin (<15 mg/dL) indicates acute malnutrition or inflammation. Critical in hospitalized patients and those recovering from surgery.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Prealbumin drops during any acute illness (it's a negative acute phase reactant). A low value during sickness doesn't necessarily mean malnutrition.

interpretation.high: Elevated in chronic renal failure (reduced catabolism), corticosteroid use, and rarely in Hodgkin lymphoma.
interpretation.low: Low prealbumin (<10 mg/dL) indicates significant protein-calorie malnutrition or acute inflammation. Predictor of postoperative complications, poor wound healing, and mortality in hospitalized patients.
interpretation.optimal: >20 mg/dL with normal CRP (to exclude inflammation as cause of low values).

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Prealbumin has a half-life of 2 days vs. albumin's 21 days — this makes it 10× more responsive to acute nutritional changes. Use prealbumin to monitor the effectiveness of nutritional interventions in ICU patients.

high.action: Discuss at next appointment
high.details: Elevated prealbumin may be seen in steroid use, high protein diets, or rarely Hodgkin disease. Usually not clinically significant.

low.details: Low prealbumin indicates acute malnutrition or inflammation (negative acute phase reactant). More sensitive to recent nutritional changes than albumin. Optimize protein and calorie intake.

optimal.details: Normal prealbumin indicates good recent nutritional status with adequate protein and calorie intake.

citations.text: Shenkin A. Serum prealbumin: Is it a marker of nutritional status or of risk of malnutrition? Clin Chem. 2006;52(12):2177-2179.
Thomas DR, Zdrowski CD, Wilson MM, et al. Malnutrition in subacute care. Am J Clin Nutr. 2002;75(2):308-313.

parameters.name: Body Mass Index
parameters.range: Underweight: <18.5. Normal: 18.5–24.9. Overweight: 25.0–29.9. Obese I: 30.0–34.9. Obese II: 35.0–39.9. Obese III: ≥40.0 kg/m².

layman.simplyPut: Your weight relative to your height — a simple screening tool for underweight, overweight, and obesity.
layman.analogy: BMI is your weight divided by height squared — a rough estimate of body fat. It's like judging a book by its cover — useful as a screening tool but doesn't tell the whole story.
layman.whatItTells: A simple index of weight-for-height calculated as weight (kg) divided by height squared (m²).

whenToWorry.worry: BMI above 30 is obese, above 40 is severely obese — both increase risk for diabetes, heart disease, and joint problems.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: BMI doesn't distinguish muscle from fat. A muscular athlete may have a 'high' BMI but be very healthy. Waist circumference is often more informative.

interpretation.high: BMI ≥30 is associated with 2–3 fold increased risk of type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, sleep apnea, osteoarthritis, and several cancers. Each 5 kg/m² increase above 25 raises all-cause mortality by 30%.
interpretation.low: BMI <18.5 increases risk of osteoporosis, infertility, immune dysfunction, and mortality. May indicate eating disorders, malabsorption, or chronic disease.
interpretation.optimal: BMI 20–25 associated with lowest all-cause mortality. Some studies suggest 22–25 is optimal for most populations.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: BMI was invented by a mathematician (Adolphe Quetelet) in the 1830s for population statistics, not individual health assessment — it misclassifies muscular individuals as overweight and doesn't capture metabolic health. Always pair with waist circumference.

high.action: See your doctor to discuss weight management
high.details: BMI 25-29.9 is overweight; ≥30 is obese. Increases risk of diabetes, heart disease, joint problems, and cancer. Target 5-10% weight loss through diet and exercise for health benefits.

low.details: BMI <18.5 is underweight, risking malnutrition, osteoporosis, immune dysfunction, and fertility problems. Evaluate for eating disorders, malabsorption, or chronic disease.

optimal.details: BMI 18.5-24.9 is normal range. Continue balanced diet and regular exercise to maintain healthy body composition.

citations.text: Jensen MD, Ryan DH, Apovian CM, et al. 2013 AHA/ACC/TOS guideline for the management of overweight and obesity in adults. Circulation. 2014;129(25 Suppl 2):S102-S138.
Slavin JL. Dietary fiber and body weight. Nutrition. 2005;21(3):411-418.

Donnelly JE, Blair SN, Jakicic JM, et al. ACSM Position Stand: Appropriate physical activity intervention strategies for weight loss and prevention of weight regain for adults. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2009;41(2):459-471.
World Health Organization. Body mass index - BMI. WHO Regional Office for Europe. Obesity and overweight fact sheet. WHO; 2021.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Normal BMI + elevated body fat + low muscle mass
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: BMI misses sarcopenic obesity; check body fat %, waist circumference, metabolic markers

drugInteractions.mechanism: Reduces appetite via hypothalamic GLP-1 receptors; delays gastric emptying
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: 10-15% body weight reduction typical; GI side effects dose-dependent

ageSpecificNotes.20s: BMI 18.5-24.9 is normal. Establish baseline and monitor trends.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: BMI naturally creeps up. Even modest weight gain (5 kg over a decade) increases diabetes risk.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Slightly higher BMI (25-27) may actually be protective in elderly (obesity paradox).
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Unintentional weight loss (BMI decline) is more concerning than mild overweight. Sarcopenic obesity (low muscle, high fat with normal BMI) is a hidden risk.

globalRanges.asian: WHO Asia-Pacific: Overweight ≥23; Obese ≥25 kg/m² (higher diabetes/CVD risk at lower BMI)
globalRanges.african: Some evidence for different body fat distribution at same BMI
globalRanges.hispanic: Higher body fat percentage at same BMI compared to European descent
globalRanges.pediatric: Use BMI-for-age percentiles: overweight ≥85th, obese ≥95th percentile
globalRanges.geriatric: BMI 23-30 may be optimal in elderly (slight overweight protective); underweight (<22) associated with mortality

parameters.name: Body Fat Percentage
parameters.range: Men: Essential fat 2–5%, Athletes 6–13%, Fitness 14–17%, Acceptable 18–24%, Obese >25%. Women: Essential fat 10–13%, Athletes 14–20%, Fitness 21–24%, Acceptable 25–31%, Obese >32%.

layman.simplyPut: What fraction of your total body weight is fat — more informative than BMI for fitness assessment.
layman.analogy: Body fat percentage tells you what fraction of your weight is fat versus lean tissue — like knowing how much of your suitcase is clothes versus packing materials.
layman.whatItTells: The proportion of total body mass that is adipose tissue.

whenToWorry.worry: Very high body fat (>32% women, >25% men) increases metabolic disease risk. Very low (<12% women, <5% men) can disrupt hormones.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Measurement methods (calipers, scales, DEXA) can vary by 3-5%. Focus on trends over time, not single measurements.

interpretation.high: Excess body fat (>25% men, >32% women) increases risk of metabolic syndrome, T2D, CVD, and certain cancers regardless of BMI. Visceral fat is more metabolically harmful than subcutaneous fat.
interpretation.low: Very low body fat causes hormonal disruption (amenorrhea in women, low testosterone in men), bone loss, immune suppression, and increased injury risk. Seen in eating disorders and overtraining.
interpretation.optimal: Men: 10–20%. Women: 18–28%. Depends on age, athletic goals, and individual health.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Metabolically healthy obesity (normal glucose, lipids, BP despite high body fat) affects up to 30% of obese individuals — but longitudinal studies show most transition to metabolically unhealthy within 10 years.

high.action: See your doctor to discuss body composition
high.details: High body fat increases metabolic disease risk even with normal BMI. Focus on exercise (both aerobic and resistance training), reduce processed foods, and increase protein intake.

low.action: Discuss with your doctor if below essential fat levels
low.details: Very low body fat (<5% men, <12% women) causes hormonal disruption, amenorrhea, immune suppression, and bone loss. Increase caloric intake and reduce excessive training.

optimal.details: Healthy body fat percentage supports hormonal balance and metabolic health. Continue balanced diet and regular exercise.

citations.text: Westerterp-Plantenga MS, Lemmens SG, Westerterp KR. Dietary protein — its role in satiety, energetics, weight loss and health. Br J Nutr. 2012;108 Suppl 2:S105-S112.
Westcott WL. Resistance training is medicine: effects of strength training on health. Curr Sports Med Rep. 2012;11(4):209-216.

Wewege M, van den Berg R, Ward RE, Keech A. The effects of high-intensity interval training vs. moderate-intensity continuous training on body composition in overweight and obese adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Obes Rev. 2017;18(6):635-646.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: High body fat + normal BMI + insulin resistance + dyslipidemia
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: TOFI (thin outside, fat inside) phenotype; visceral fat assessment key

drugInteractions.mechanism: Promotes visceral adipogenesis and lipogenesis; Cushing-like fat distribution
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump with chronic use

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Healthy range: Men 10-20%, Women 18-28%. Athletes may be lower.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Body fat tends to increase even without weight gain due to muscle loss. Resistance training counteracts this.
ageSpecificNotes.60s: Redistribution from subcutaneous to visceral fat increases metabolic risk.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Very low body fat (<15% men, <22% women) may indicate malnutrition or sarcopenia.

globalRanges.asian: Higher body fat at same BMI; obesity threshold may be 25-27% (men) and 33-35% (women) vs Western 25%/32%

parameters.name: Waist Circumference
parameters.range: Men: <94 cm (<37 inches) normal, 94–102 cm increased risk, >102 cm (>40 inches) substantially increased risk. Women: <80 cm (<31.5 inches) normal, 80–88 cm increased risk, >88 cm (>35 inches) substantially increased risk.

layman.simplyPut: The distance around your belly at the navel — excess belly fat strongly predicts heart disease and diabetes risk.
layman.analogy: Waist circumference is a simple tape measure around your belly — it's actually a better predictor of metabolic disease than BMI because belly fat (visceral fat) is the most dangerous type.
layman.whatItTells: The circumference of the abdomen measured at the midpoint between the lower rib margin and the iliac crest.

whenToWorry.worry: Above 40 inches (102 cm) in men or 35 inches (88 cm) in women significantly increases diabetes and heart disease risk.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Measure at the same time of day, at your navel level. Small variations with meals and time of day are normal.

interpretation.high: Elevated waist circumference indicates visceral adiposity. Men >102 cm or women >88 cm have 2–4 fold increased risk of T2D, CVD, and metabolic syndrome. Visceral fat produces inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) and free fatty acids that drive insulin resistance.
interpretation.low: Very low waist circumference may indicate underweight or low muscle mass.
interpretation.optimal: Men: <94 cm. Women: <80 cm. Waist-to-height ratio <0.5 is also a useful target.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Waist circumference measured at the umbilicus (not the narrowest point) is the best correlate of visceral adipose tissue measured by CT — it adds cardiovascular risk prediction beyond BMI.

high.action: See your doctor to discuss metabolic risk
high.details: Waist >40 inches (men) or >35 inches (women) indicates central obesity with high cardiometabolic risk. Visceral fat is more dangerous than subcutaneous. Target waist reduction through diet and exercise.

low.details: Low waist circumference is healthy and indicates low visceral fat. Ensure adequate nutrition if very lean.

optimal.details: Normal waist circumference indicates low visceral fat and reduced metabolic syndrome risk.

citations.text: Estruch R, Ros E, Salas-Salvadó J, et al. Primary prevention of cardiovascular disease with a Mediterranean diet supplemented with extra-virgin olive oil or nuts. N Engl J Med. 2018;378(25):e34.

Stanhope KL, Schwarz JM, Keim NL, et al. Consuming fructose-sweetened, not glucose-sweetened, beverages increases visceral adiposity and lipids and decreases insulin sensitivity in overweight/obese humans. J Clin Invest. 2009;119(5):1322-1334.
Vissers D, Hens W, Brouwers J, et al. The effect of exercise on visceral adipose tissue in overweight adults: a systematic review and meta-analysis. PLoS One. 2013;8(2):e56415.
World Health Organization. Waist circumference and waist-hip ratio: report of a WHO expert consultation. WHO; 2008.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Elevated waist circumference + TG >= 150 + HDL < 40(M)/50(F)
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: 3 of 5 ATP III criteria: waist, TG, HDL, BP, fasting glucose

drugInteractions.mechanism: Preferential visceral fat reduction
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: Waist reduction correlates with cardiometabolic improvement

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Establish baseline. Waist circumference is the simplest measure of visceral fat.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Visceral fat accumulation accelerates, especially in men and post-menopausal women.
ageSpecificNotes.70plus: Waist circumference remains predictive of metabolic risk in elderly, though BMI may be less reliable.

globalRanges.who: Men ≥94 cm, Women ≥80 cm (increased risk); Men ≥102 cm, Women ≥88 cm (substantially increased risk)
globalRanges.asian: Lower cutoffs: Men ≥90 cm, Women ≥80 cm (IDF criteria for Asians); Japanese: Men ≥85 cm, Women ≥90 cm

parameters.name: Lean Body Mass
parameters.range: Varies by sex, height, and age. Appendicular skeletal muscle mass index (ASMI = appendicular lean mass / height²): Men: >7.0 kg/m². Women: >5.5 kg/m².

layman.simplyPut: Your total weight minus body fat — includes muscles, bones, organs, and water.
layman.analogy: Lean body mass is everything in your body that isn't fat — muscles, bones, organs, water. It's the metabolically active part of you that burns calories even at rest.
layman.whatItTells: Total body weight minus fat mass, comprising muscle, bone, water, organs, and connective tissue.

whenToWorry.worry: Declining lean mass with aging (sarcopenia) increases fall risk, metabolic disease, and disability. Resistance training is the best prevention.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Lean mass naturally decreases with age, but the decline can be significantly slowed with regular strength training and adequate protein.

interpretation.high: Higher lean mass is protective — associated with higher metabolic rate, better glucose disposal, greater functional capacity, and lower mortality.
interpretation.low: Low lean mass (sarcopenia) predicts disability, falls, fractures, metabolic syndrome, and all-cause mortality independent of fat mass. Sarcopenic obesity (high fat + low muscle) is particularly dangerous.
interpretation.optimal: Above median for age and sex with adequate muscle function (strength and performance).

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Sarcopenic obesity (low muscle + high fat) is the worst phenotype for metabolic health and mortality — these patients have normal BMI but high body fat and insulin resistance. Identify them with DEXA or BIA.

high.action: No action needed — high lean mass is protective
high.details: High lean body mass indicates good muscle development. Associated with better metabolic health and longevity.

low.action: Start resistance training and increase protein intake
low.details: Low lean mass (sarcopenia) increases fall risk, insulin resistance, and mortality. Begin progressive resistance training 2-3 times/week with 1.2-1.6 g protein/kg/day.

optimal.details: Normal lean body mass supports metabolism, functional independence, and overall health.

citations.text: Morton RW, Murphy KT, McKellar SR, et al. A systematic review, meta-analysis and meta-regression of the effect of protein supplementation on resistance training-induced gains in muscle mass and strength in healthy adults. Br J Sports Med. 2018;52(6):376-384.

Kreider RB, Kalman DS, Antonio J, et al. International Society of Sports Nutrition position stand: safety and efficacy of creatine supplementation in exercise, sport, and medicine. J Int Soc Sports Nutr. 2017;14:18.

Schoenfeld BJ, Ogborn D, Krieger JW. Dose-response relationship between weekly resistance training volume and increases in muscle mass: A systematic review and meta-analysis. J Sports Sci. 2017;35(11):1073-1082.

parameters.name: Visceral Fat Area
parameters.range: CT/MRI: VFA <100 cm² (low risk), 100–130 cm² (moderate), >130 cm² (high risk). BIA visceral fat level: 1–9 (normal), 10–14 (high), ≥15 (very high).

layman.simplyPut: The hidden fat packed around your organs deep in your belly — the most dangerous type of fat for health.
layman.analogy: Visceral fat is the hidden fat wrapped around your internal organs — you can't pinch it. It's metabolically active and releases inflammatory chemicals, making it the most dangerous type of fat.
layman.whatItTells: Fat stored around abdominal organs (liver, intestines, pancreas) that is metabolically active and pathogenic.

whenToWorry.worry: High visceral fat dramatically increases risk of type 2 diabetes, heart disease, and fatty liver — even in people who look thin on the outside (TOFI: thin outside, fat inside).
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Visceral fat responds well to aerobic exercise and dietary changes — it's often the first fat to go with lifestyle modifications.

interpretation.high: VFA >100 cm² associated with 2–5 fold increased cardiometabolic risk. Visceral fat has direct portal vein drainage to liver, causing hepatic insulin resistance and steatosis.
interpretation.low: Low visceral fat is protective. Can be achieved through exercise even without overall weight loss.
interpretation.optimal: VFA <100 cm². BIA visceral fat level <10.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: One hour of moderate aerobic exercise preferentially reduces visceral fat even without weight loss — this is why exercise improves metabolic markers before the scale changes.

high.action: See your doctor within 1-2 weeks
high.details: High visceral fat is the most metabolically dangerous fat type — increases insulin resistance, inflammation, cardiovascular disease, and cancer risk. Aerobic exercise and dietary changes are most effective.

low.details: Low visceral fat is metabolically favorable and reduces cardiometabolic risk.

optimal.details: Normal visceral fat area supports metabolic health. Aerobic exercise is particularly effective at reducing visceral fat.

citations.text: Hairston KG, Vitolins MZ, Norris JM, et al. Lifestyle factors and 5-year abdominal fat accumulation in a minority cohort: the IRAS Family Study. Obesity. 2012;20(2):421-427.
Ohkawara K, Tanaka S, Miyachi M, et al. A dose-response relation between aerobic exercise and visceral fat reduction: systematic review of clinical trials. Int J Obes. 2007;31(12):1786-1797.

parameters.name: Waist-to-Hip Ratio
parameters.range: Men: <0.90 (low risk), 0.90–0.99 (moderate), ≥1.0 (high risk). Women: <0.80 (low risk), 0.80–0.85 (moderate), >0.85 (high risk).

layman.simplyPut: Your waist measurement divided by your hip measurement — shows whether you carry fat around your belly or hips.
layman.analogy: Waist-hip ratio compares your waist to your hips — apple-shaped (more belly fat) carries higher health risk than pear-shaped (more hip/thigh fat).
layman.whatItTells: The ratio of waist circumference to hip circumference, indicating body fat distribution.

whenToWorry.worry: Ratio above 0.90 in men or 0.85 in women indicates central obesity and increased cardiovascular risk.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Body shape is partly genetic. Even if you can't change your shape entirely, reducing waist circumference through exercise improves health outcomes.

interpretation.high: High WHR indicates central fat accumulation — associated with 2–3 fold increased risk of myocardial infarction (INTERHEART study). Apple-shaped fat distribution is metabolically harmful.
interpretation.low: Low WHR indicates peripheral (gluteofemoral) fat distribution, which may be metabolically protective. Pear-shaped phenotype has lower cardiometabolic risk.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Waist-to-hip ratio captures both visceral fat accumulation (waist) and gluteal muscle/fat loss (hip), making it a better mortality predictor than either measurement alone — an apple shape is deadlier than a pear shape.

high.action: See your doctor to discuss metabolic risk
high.details: WHR >0.90 (men) or >0.85 (women) indicates central obesity and increased cardiovascular and metabolic risk. Focus on aerobic exercise and reducing visceral fat.

low.details: Low waist-to-hip ratio indicates healthy fat distribution, typically pear-shaped which is metabolically favorable.

optimal.details: Healthy WHR indicates good fat distribution and lower cardiometabolic risk.

citations.text: Yusuf S, Hawken S, Ounpuu S, et al. Obesity and the risk of myocardial infarction in 27,000 participants from 52 countries: a case-control study. Lancet. 2005;366(9497):1640-1649.
Vissers D, Hens W, Brouwers J, et al. The effect of exercise on visceral adipose tissue in overweight adults. PLoS One. 2013;8(2):e56415.
World Health Organization. Waist circumference and waist-hip ratio: report of a WHO expert consultation. WHO; 2008.

parameters.name: Bone Mineral Content
parameters.range: Total body BMC: Men 2,500–3,500 g, Women 2,000–2,800 g. Varies with body size and ethnicity.

layman.simplyPut: The total amount of minerals (mainly calcium) in your bones — more minerals mean stronger bones.
layman.analogy: Bone mineral content measures the total amount of minerals (mostly calcium) in your bones — like weighing the concrete in a building's foundation. More minerals = stronger bones.
layman.whatItTells: The total amount of mineral (primarily calcium hydroxyapatite) in the skeleton, measured in grams.

whenToWorry.worry: Low bone mineral content increases fracture risk, especially in postmenopausal women and elderly men.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Bone mineral content is related to bone density but also reflects bone size. Larger people naturally have higher BMC.

interpretation.high: Higher BMC is protective against fractures. Achievable through nutrition, weight-bearing exercise, and adequate hormonal status during growth years.
interpretation.low: Low BMC indicates reduced skeletal mineral reserve and increased fracture risk. Small-framed, thin, sedentary individuals and those with osteoporosis risk factors should monitor.
interpretation.optimal: Above age- and sex-matched mean. Maximizing peak BMC before age 30 is the best fracture prevention strategy.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Bone is living tissue that responds to mechanical loading — the principle of 'use it or lose it' applies literally. Astronauts lose 1-2% bone per month in microgravity, equivalent to a decade of age-related bone loss.

high.action: No action needed
high.details: High bone mineral content indicates strong, dense bones. Continue weight-bearing exercise and adequate calcium/vitamin D.

low.details: Low bone mineral content increases fracture risk. Start weight-bearing exercise, ensure calcium 1000-1200 mg/day and vitamin D 800-1000 IU/day. May need DEXA scan and medication.

optimal.details: Normal bone mineral content supports skeletal strength. Maintain weight-bearing exercise and adequate calcium/vitamin D.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Calcium and Vitamin D. National Academies Press; 2011.
Kohrt WM, Bloomfield SA, Little KD, Nelson ME, Yingling VR. ACSM Position Stand: Physical activity and bone health. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2004;36(11):1985-1996.

parameters.name: Total Body Water
parameters.range: Men: 55–65% of body weight. Women: 45–55% of body weight.

layman.simplyPut: How much of your body weight is water — about 60% in men and 50% in women is normal.
layman.analogy: Your body is about 60% water — it fills every cell, cushions organs, and carries nutrients. Total body water measurement shows if you're properly hydrated or running dry.
layman.whatItTells: The total volume of water in the body, comprising intracellular fluid (ICF, ~60% of TBW) and extracellular fluid (ECF, ~40%).

whenToWorry.worry: Dehydration (low TBW) impairs every body system — kidneys, brain, heart. Overhydration can dangerously dilute sodium.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: TBW varies with age, sex, and body composition. Drinking when thirsty is usually sufficient for most healthy people.

interpretation.high: Excessive TBW may indicate fluid overload (heart failure, renal failure, liver cirrhosis). Edema and weight gain are clinical signs.
interpretation.low: Low TBW (<50% in men, <45% in women) may indicate chronic dehydration or high body fat percentage. Acute dehydration >2% impairs cognitive and physical performance; >5% is dangerous; >10% is life-threatening.
interpretation.optimal: 55–60% of body weight for men, 50–55% for women, with adequate daily fluid intake.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Thirst is already a late sign of dehydration in the elderly — by the time an older adult feels thirsty, they may already be 2-3% dehydrated, which impairs cognition and increases fall risk. Proactive hydration is essential.

high.action: See your doctor if edema present
high.details: Elevated total body water may indicate fluid retention from heart failure, kidney disease, or liver cirrhosis. Check for peripheral edema, ascites, and shortness of breath.

low.action: Increase fluid intake; see doctor if persistent
low.details: Low total body water indicates dehydration. Elderly and children are most vulnerable. Chronic dehydration affects kidney function and cognitive performance.

optimal.details: Normal body water content supports organ function and metabolism. Drink water regularly throughout the day.

citations.text: Institute of Medicine. Dietary Reference Intakes for Water, Potassium, Sodium, Chloride, and Sulfate. National Academies Press; 2005.
Sawka MN, Burke LM, Eichner ER, et al. ACSM Position Stand: Exercise and fluid replacement. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2007;39(2):377-390.

parameters.name: Menstrual Cycle Tracking
parameters.range: Cycle length: 21–35 days; Flow duration: 3–7 days; Blood loss: 5–80 mL per cycle (>80 mL = heavy menstrual bleeding)

layman.simplyPut: Tracking your period's timing, length, and symptoms — irregular cycles may signal hormonal imbalances.
layman.analogy: The menstrual cycle is your body's monthly preparation for potential pregnancy — like a monthly renovation project where the uterine lining is built up, checked, and either used (pregnancy) or reset (period).
layman.whatItTells: Menstrual cycle tracking monitors cycle length, regularity, flow duration, and associated symptoms as vital indicators of reproductive and overall health.

whenToWorry.worry: Cycles shorter than 21 days, longer than 35 days, very heavy bleeding, or no periods (amenorrhea) need evaluation.
whenToWorry.dontWorry: Cycle length varies by 1-5 days from month to month. Cycles are often irregular in the first few years after menarche and near menopause.

interpretation.high: Heavy menstrual bleeding (>80 mL or >7 days) or frequent cycles (<21 days): evaluate for fibroids, polyps, adenomyosis, coagulopathy (von Willebrand disease), thyroid disorders, or endometrial pathology.
interpretation.low: Absent periods (amenorrhea) or infrequent cycles (>35 days — oligomenorrhea): evaluate for pregnancy, PCOS, thyroid dysfunction, hyperprolactinemia, hypothalamic amenorrhea (stress, weight loss, excessive exercise), or premature ovarian insufficiency.
interpretation.optimal: Regular cycles of 26–32 days with 3–5 days of moderate flow and minimal disruptive symptoms.

quickRef.clinicalPearl: Menstrual cycle irregularity is a vital sign — the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists now recommends treating the menstrual cycle as the 'fifth vital sign' in adolescents and young women.

high.action: See your gynecologist within 1-2 weeks
high.details: Irregular, long (>35 days), or absent cycles may indicate PCOS, thyroid disorders, premature ovarian insufficiency, or hypothalamic amenorrhea. Needs hormonal evaluation.

low.details: Very short cycles (<21 days) or frequent bleeding may indicate anovulation, fibroids, polyps, or hormonal imbalance. Needs evaluation.

optimal.details: Regular 21-35 day cycles indicate healthy hormonal function. Continue tracking for fertility awareness and health monitoring.

citations.text: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists. Menstruation in Girls and Adolescents: Using the Menstrual Cycle as a Vital Sign. ACOG Committee Opinion No. 651. Obstet Gynecol. 2015;126(6):e143-e146.

differentialDiagnosis.pattern: Irregular cycles + hyperandrogenism + polycystic ovaries
differentialDiagnosis.keyDistinguisher: Rotterdam criteria; most common cause of anovulatory infertility

drugInteractions.mechanism: Suppresses ovulation via HPO axis negative feedback; provides withdrawal bleed
drugInteractions.clinicalNote: First-line for cycle regulation in PCOS; also reduces androgens via SHBG increase

ageSpecificNotes.20s: Normal cycle: 24-38 days. Irregular cycles in 20s: evaluate for PCOS, thyroid disease, or hypothalamic amenorrhea.
ageSpecificNotes.30s: Peak reproductive years. Cycle regularity supports fertility assessment.
ageSpecificNotes.40s: Perimenopause begins. Shorter and more variable cycles are expected.
ageSpecificNotes.50s: Average menopause age 51. Menopause is diagnosed after 12 months of amenorrhea.
